Hi kkvilekval,

While admittedly a bit confusing initially, this is expected behavior.

The host of `'http://:9021/'` is empty. As such, `join()` adopts the empty
string as the new host.

For context, this is the behavior of Python's `urllib.parse.urljoin()`, too.

```python
>>> from urllib.parse import urljoin
>>> urljoin('http://domain.com:9999', 'http://:9021/')
'http://:9021/'
```

And even other language's standard libraries, like Node's.

```javascript
$ node
> url.resolve('http://domain.com:9999/', 'http://:9021/')
'http://:9021/'
```

Back to your original example. If you want to set the port of an existing URL
to, the easiest way to do that is with `set()`.

```python
>>> from furl import furl
>>> furl('http://domain.com:9999').set(port=furl('http://:9021/').port).url
'http://domain.com:9021'
```

Or, to preserve the original host, join on a URL with that same host.

```python
>>> from furl import furl
>>> furl('http://domain.com:9999').join('http://domain.com:9021/').url
'http://domain.com:9021'
```

Does that answer your question?

Hey Wong, I was fooling around with your implementation, and I love it! It definitely satisfies my use case. It's even easy to map multiple keys to a single callback, just as you did for `KEYS_ENTER`, `KEYS_UP`, and `KEYS_DOWN`.

```py

# ...

```

I found it interesting that you pass `self` to the custom_handler so that the handler has access to the `picker` instance. It will allow clients to do fancy stuff with the callback.

I fixed the unicode issue in my upload field. I created a custom `UploadedField` to fix my problem, sanitizing the filename with `werkzeug.secure_filename`. I'm using Flask, so `werkzeug` is already a dependency for me.

```
from depot.fields.sqlalchemy import UploadedFile, UploadedFileField
from werkzeug import secure_filename

class SafeUploadedFile(UploadedFile):
    def process_content(self, content, filename=None, content_type=None):
        if not filename:
            filename = content.filename
        filename = secure_filename(filename)
        super(SafeUploadedFile, self).process_content(content, filename, content_type)


# my model attribute
myfile = db.Column(UploadedFileField(upload_type=SafeUploadedFile))
```

You must sanitize filenames. It is a common attack vector. Maybe depot can do it built-in.

@picca

How does setting the FISX_DOC_DIR environment variable affects debian?

The goal of those two variables (FISX_DOC_DIR and FISX_DATA_DIR) was that maintainers could specify where things should be found.

I would expect if those are set, everything is reproducible and chosen by maintainers and if they are not set then the module handle the installation of data and documentation in the module default way (installation together with the installed module).

But the environment variable was thought to be used to set the final destination. If you would want to test things using via that way, you would have to set the destination environment variable, perform the build, change the environment variable, perform the tests, get rid of the environment variable and build the package. Now that DataDir.py works as it was supposed to do, you only set the environment variable  to set the destination and the tests will run from the build directory as far is it is somewhere down the directory where setup.py is.

I thought Debian was against the use of environment variables. I remember I had to change things in the past in order to avoid that. If it is no longer the case, it costs me nothing to check for the existence of the environment variables at run time.

About Iandscape: short answer is that - it is okay to have multiple lines.  `max-line-length` the maximum allowed line length after which landscape will complain. But the guiding principle is to have as many lines as necessary to make the code readable. Having complex logic in one line will, most times, make it hard to read. In some cases, there is a speed advantage of having long lines (eg list comprehensions, etc). But most times, there is no speedup. This is the case with most projects, not just with dpkt. 

About the additional `int` : it is probably not going to hurt as much on a L7 module - as it gets called much less often than a L3 module's code. However, we might as well fix this, as we know about it.

Just thought I would also mention (in case other people stumble on this) this rule also applies to indexing files.
For example: I have indexed a 12GB fasta file with default arguments
```
minimap2 -d db.mmi db.fa
```
When running `minimap2` later with this index file, changing the `-I` flag has no effect on how the index is loaded in:
```
minimap2 -aI 64g db.mmi in.fa 
```
Will still load the index into memory in 4GB chunks (default).

So to avoid this I found I had to index like so
```
minimap2 -I 64g -d db.mmi db.fa
```

I agree that this seems very arbitrary, but the only solution is to enforce different variable names.

Unless it's an inline image (very rare), there will be a reference to the dict key for the image XObject inside the content stream, but the image XObject does not itself reside in `page.Contents`; rather it resides in the `page.Resources.XObject` dictionary.

So, you could search in `page`, or `page.Resources`, or `page.Resources.XObject`, but if you search in `page.Contents`, you are looking in the wrong place.

If by "magic" you mean the nofollow stuff, that's just to keep the `obj.iteritems` from being stuck in a loop -- each page object references its parent...

That's awesome!  I won't be able to look seriously at it until next weekend, but it's new functionality that will be great to have.

Four things that I see at a glance:

1) The `PdfDict` class is designed to match closely to a dict in an actual PDF file, so one of the things it does is return `None` if a key does not exist (because semantically, a PDF None is the same as a key not existing in a PDF file.)  So in general, there's no real reason for `get`.

2) The old-style division operator may not work with Python 3.

3) I don't think you want a `PdfName.None`; that would actually be the string `'/None'` which I've never seen used anywhere.  pdfrw matches the regular Python None to the PDF file `'None'`.

4) Most PDFs are not encrypted, and I would prefer a bit of refactoring to minimize the speed hit on unencrypted files.

So, for the dict code, for example:

`cfm = params.get(PdfName.CFM, PdfName.None)`

should probably be:

`cfm = params.CFM`

and:

`key_size = int(doc.Encrypt.get(PdfName.Length, 40)) / 8`

could be:

`key_size = int(doc.Encrypt.Length or 40) // 8`

As you can see, there's probably no real reason to modify `PdfDict.get()` -- the code is shorter and quite readable without it.

And for the encryption, if you could make sure that there's no attempt to run decryption code unless we're decrypting the PDF, that would be great.

trying to demonstrate would be a good thing (most people that find your
examples will probably only have a fuzzy idea about what this whole
file-descriptor passing thing is anyway... it's pretty esoteric). Beyond
that, you might consider having the client use os.pipe() to create a pair

print  the "Hello World" string the server writes to the fd. Sure, it's a
blocking operation but the rest of Twisted blocks on file descriptor reads
anyway (... which there doesn't seem to be any good way around at present).

It's happening because you didn't include annotations for every part.  The data loading routines then set those parts to OBJECT_PART_NOT_PRESENT, which just makes parts with a certain really huge point location.  That would be fine and the training code would do the right thing, except the flipping code in that file that you have doesn't do the right thing.  When it flips points with x,y values of OBJECT_PART_NOT_PRESENT it messes them up and turns them into some really huge different point which then goes on to derail the entire rest of the code.  

You should label all the points.

Our product uses pyinstaller to package up the Python into an exe so the
paths are a little weird looking. The error only happens when the product
is packaged up by Pyinstaller. When I ran it from a Python file it worked
fine. As an experiment I changed the line in core.py.

from . import backend
  ->
from .backend import KeyringBackend, get_all_keyring

This worked! keyring loaded but then failed a little later. The code in
backend.py died in _load_backends when kwallet was not found. The code
fooled the analysis of pyinstaller. I worked around this by adding an
unneeded import in a random file.

from keyring.backends import OS_X, SecretService, Windows, kwallet

This prompted pyInstaller to include those files in the final product.
Great, now everything works except if the user used the option
for EncryptedKeyring. I had the following code:

from keyrings.alt.Windows import EncryptedKeyring

yet, the class wasn't loaded. It turned out that by adding an empty
__init__.py to keyrings I could load it. This might be pyinstaller being a
little too picky about imports from directories instead of packages.

Finally, after I undid the changes to core.py, but left the workaround of
the dummy importing of the 4 backends, things still worked. Perhaps there
is another workaround to get the EncryptedKeyring import to work without
adding the __init__.py to keyrings.

-Dave

Namecheap's "sandbox" API is global, so the made up `lexicontest` domain is locked into the account/credentials of that user.  The way the tests are setup with hardcoded domains, everyone who needs to hit the "real" API (new functionality, edits, testing a library change) must edit the test file to change the domain (in addition to deleting the cassettes). 

Using a class property, like in my example, means developers wouldn't have to edit the test file to customize it -- it would be all run locally.

Eventually this library will likely be on a CI build that doesn't just check against the cassette recordings, but against live services.  For that to happen, you'll need the ability to customize the domain in each provider test.

Yes, I opened this PR specifically for Gandi, as several users could not enable the REST protocol on Gandi provider only with environment variables.

But this is a more general issue about design around environment variable.

For you solution 1,it is the most obvious answer, but there will be difficulties in understanding which env var name to use for what. For instance, `--api-protocol` would resolve to `LEXICON_GANDI_API_PROTOCOL` and `--auth-token` to `LEXICON_GANDI_TOKEN`. And about options not related to a provider, like `--delegate`?

About the option 2, it is quite explicit, but it is not an implementation at the lexicon level, each developer which uses lexicon will need to do something.

I would like to give you a third solution: `LEXICON_OPTIONS` and `LEXICON_[PROVIDER]_OPTIONS`. For the first one, if specified, options are always added to lexicon, and we can set generic options like so `LEXICON_OPTIONS='--delegate=example.com --whatever=test'`. For the second one, options are added to lexicon when the given provider is used, and we can set provider specific options like so `LEXICON_GANDI_OPTIONS='--api-protocol=rest --whatever-specific=test'`.

Version 0.5 has been uploaded to the Python package index. You can install or upgrade it with pip. Note that the import syntax for the package has changed. You should now use:

    import jsonxs
    
    data = {'foo': 1}
    jsonxs.jsonxs(data, 'bar', jsonxs.ACTION_SET, 2)

Or optionally:

```
def diagnose_mode():
    print ssh_connection.base_prompt
    result = ""
    if re.search("\-diagnose", ssh_connection.base_prompt):
	pass
    else:
	ssh_connection.config_mode()
	ssh_connection.base_prompt += "-diagnose"
	result = ssh_connection.send_command("diagnose",expect_string=ssh_connection.base_prompt)
    print ssh_connection.base_prompt
    return result

def exit_diagnose_mode():
    print ssh_connection.base_prompt
    result = ""
    if re.search("\-diagnose", ssh_connection.base_prompt):
	ssh_connection.base_prompt = ssh_connection.base_prompt.replace('-diagnose', '')
	result = ssh_connection.send_command("quit",expect_string=ssh_connection.base_prompt)
	ssh_connection.exit_config_mode()
    else:
	pass
    print ssh_connection.base_prompt
    return result

```

Hi @minrk,

the case is when the driver is running from a machine whose Python is, for example, located in `~./conda/envs/myenv/python`, while the cluster has Python located in `/anaconda/bin/python`, i.e. not in an environment.

The fact that the driver started using `~./conda/envs/myenv/python`, does not mean that the cluster can't use `/anaconda/bin/python` once starting doing stuff. Also because nothing is started in the cluster before executing `SparkSession.builder.getOrCreate()`.

The workflow looks like this

```python
import findspark
findspark.init(…)  # here PYSPARK_PYTHON gets set
import pyspark
spark  = pyspark.sql.SparkSession.builder.getOrCreate()  # here PYSPARK_PYTHON is used. It doesn't have to be sys.executable
```

Moreover, I don't think there's anything "harmful" about the change. Above all, I think, it matches the expectation that, setting `PYSPARK_PYTHON`, is enough to tell Spark where to find python on the workers.

I have found the issue after attempting to create a virtualenv with the nodeenv. The issue is that my partition that I was installing the nodeenv into was mounted with `rw,user,auto` options and the "user" option infers "noexec" which prevents virtualenv or nodeenv from execution. 

The solution is to add the "exec" option and remount the partition. I've tested and verified that a `--prebuilt` and from source both install and a `node -v` returns the nodeenv version after activation.

attribution: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/14759549/strange-permissions-problems-for-virtualenv-python-executable-lubuntu-12-10 (the accepted answer by duncanm)

I have a couple of things for your consideration. I am no expert on this, these encoding issues are problematic. I think you really have several problems to deal with.

1. Interpreting the strings you get from the underlying libraries you are using (C extension code).
2. Providing those strings to consumers of psutil so that they can use them properly.
3. Accepting strings from consumers for things like filtering or getting a process by name etc.

Of course, problems 2 and 3 are trivially solved once the harder problem 1 is addressed. So what you really want is a way to ensure that strings are always represented using the same method throughout psutil. This means you want to encode/decode strings "at the edge" of your code base. You probably want to deal with them internally as unicode (or utf-8, which would involve re-encoding).

Exhibit.

Take for example the `re` module. You can use either bytes or unicode for patterns, but it requires that the data being processed uses the same representation.

```python
>>> import re
>>> bs = b'Bytes data.'
>>> us = u'Unicode data.'
>>> re.search(b'data', bs)
<_sre.SRE_Match object; span=(6, 10), match=b'data'>
>>> re.search(b'data', us)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/re.py", line 173, in search
    return _compile(pattern, flags).search(string)
TypeError: cannot use a bytes pattern on a string-like object
```

This is convenient because I control both parameters. This leaves the encoding/decoding issues in my hands.

Now let's take a look at a fake example involving psutil. Let's say that there is a function in psutil that allows me to check if a process with a given name exists `get_process(name)`. Now let's suppose that the C extension code for OSX returns unicode process names but utf8 on Windows. As a user, I only control the name I wish to search for. Probably the easiest way for you to handle this is to ensure user input is immediately converted to unicode (or functions only accept unicode). Further, the C extension code must convert to unicode as soon as data is obtained from the underlying API (Win32 API or similar). Then all of your internal comparison/filtering etc. can be done implicitly since all strings are unicode.

Take a look at this piece of code:

https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/master/psutil/arch/osx/process_info.c#L180

vs.

https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/master/psutil/arch/windows/process_info.c#L683

If the first example, you are using the python version to dictate how to decode the string. But in the second, you know (because of MSDN documentation) the encoding that is in use. The second example is the pattern you want to follow. The API you rely on will dictate the encoding being used (which may be configured globally on the system). You should basically use that information to decode everything to unicode and then deal with that internally.

The command given to --tree-filter is interpreted by bash. Therefore, it works like this:
(Tested in _Anaconda Prompt_ on Win10 which is a Windows command prompt with nbstripout/Python available but not having GIT in its %PATH%)

`"c:\Program Files\Git\bin\git.exe" filter-branch -f --tree-filter "(IFS=$'\n'; for fn in $(find . -type f -name '*.ipynb'); do nbstripout \"$fn\" --keep-count --keep-output; done)"`

The most outside parentheses (aka subshell) are required to encapsulate the **IFS** setting for supporting file names with spaces. Changing the **IFS** breaks the GIT filter-branch command otherwise.

Here's an example using [drkjam/netaddr](https://github.com/drkjam/netaddr) for ease of explanation and because I'm writing a class that utilities both libraries in concert to do something similar to my below example.

```python
###########################################################################
# The following example will filter traffic on eth0 and send traffic from #
# network 192.168.0.0/24 to handle 1:20 and 172.16.0.0/26 to handle 1:30  #
###########################################################################
from netaddr import IPNetwork
from pyroute2 import IPRoute

####################
# Setup Parameters #
####################
iface_name = 'eth0'

network1 = '192.168.0.0/24'
network2 = '172.16.0.0/26'

parent = 0x10000
handle1 = 0x20
handle2 = 0x30

TC_FILTER_OFFSET_SRCNET = 12
TC_FILTER_OFFSET_DSTNET = 16

#################
# Pre-Execution #
#################
ip = IPRoute()

iface_index = ip.link_lookup(ifname=iface_name)[0]
handle1 += parent
handle2 += parent

lan1 = IPNetwork(network1)
lan2 = IPNetwork(network2)

filter_key_src = "{network}/{mask}+{offset}".format(
    network=hex(lan1.network),
    mask=hex(lan1.netmask),
    offset=TC_FILTER_OFFSET_SRCNET)

filter_key_dst = "{network}/{mask}+{offset}".format(
    network=hex(lan2.network),
    mask=hex(lan2.netmask),
    offset=TC_FILTER_OFFSET_DSTNET)

#############
# Execution #
#############
ip.tc("add-filter", "u32", iface_index,
    parent=parent,
    prio=10,
    protocol=socket.AF_INET,
    target=handle1,
    keys=[filter_key_src])

ip.tc("add-filter", "u32", iface_index,
    parent=parent,
    prio=10,
    protocol=socket.AF_INET,
    target=handle2,
    keys=[filter_key_dst])
```

I would merge a version check timeout config!

I have somewhat unique views on version checking in the Kafka community. There is a long running debate on the dev list about how to make this easier, see KIP-35. No other clients have been willing to take the approach I've used here, mai ly because the sercer behavior is not well defined, well understood or well tested in this space. The official client requires users to manually verify that their client library is older than their server. They generally do not go back and improve old clients -- you must upgrade server for improvements.

I think the community is better served if a single client can operate with older brokers relatively seamlessly (minus certain features of course). I am working w librdkafka and others to convince server team to officially support a method to get the broker version dynamically. If that gets merged, I plan to switch check_version to a more sane approach that would be able to reasonably handle network latency issues (at least for all newer brokers)

This is not specific to Whitenoise, see https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/29069.

Django relies on the ```request_finished``` signal to release the DB connection, if one was requested during the processing of the request. When a request is served by Whitenoise this signal is not sent so if a previous middleware has made ORM requests the DB connection will not be closed. The same thing happens with the static and media file serving of Django itself, for example.

Variables exposed to testinfra should be the same variables that ansible converged against.  In our case we have a [custom vars plugin very similar to ursula's](https://github.com/blueboxgroup/ursula/blob/master/plugins/vars/default_vars.py).  The only difference it loads defaults from a directory.

When we converge ansible, we converge ansible with the following env vars, which basically allows ansible to merge the current inventory with a set of defaults.

```
    ANSIBLE_VAR_DEFAULTS_GLOB: ../../../inventory/fusion/metapod/defaults/*.yml
    ANSIBLE_VARS_PLUGINS: ../../../plugins/vars
```

If testinfra were to expose ansible vars inside the tests, it would need to load the vars the same way ansible loaded them.  However, if we were to have this functionality, I could really leverage testinfra against multiple deployed environments, and generisize my tests quite a bit.

sql = "INSERT INTO #tmp (txt) VALUES (?)"

crsr.setinputsizes([(pyodbc.SQL_WVARCHAR, 50, 0)])

```

I actually have a bigger idea for this module, instead of allowing only `ip`, `country` and `countryCode` I want to allow any format, that is available in the JSON of `url_geo`. The current code is very bound to the specific format of `http://ip-api.com/json` (where `ip` is available under the `query` key, `countryCode` is camel case, etc), what I want instead is to allow using any URL and using any format that is available in the JSON of that url.

For example, I found out that `http://ip-api.com/json` have DNS issues and it is not always available for me, but the alternative site `https://freegeoip.net/json/` works great. However, they have different format keys than `http://ip-api.com/json`:

- `ip` instead of `query`
- `country_name` instead of `country`
- `country_code` instead of `countryCode`

So really even though currently plugin allows using different URLs, you can't use any other URL because `query`, `country` and now `countryCode` are hardcoded. So I want to allow any format options.

For me, when I need to warp a MIDI file's timing to an arbitrary timebase, I find adjusting the time of the events and discarding tempo information (see `adjust_times`) works best.  The other option would be to insert lots and lots of tempo change events, but that would get totally insane for smoothly varying timebases pretty quickly, so I decided not to do that.  Of course, it would be nice in general to be able to tell pretty_midi that there should be a new single tempo change at time x to tempo y and have it adjust everything's timing.  It would just get kind of hairy to implement, I think, which is why I haven't done it yet.  In general, it would be nice to have a way to quickly retrieve time-in-seconds of different events while still maintaining ticks/bar position, but that's also pretty hairy to implement..!

Hi,

As stated in other ticket, the `Sizers` concept is still an underdeveloped one. Obviously not having the `data` (which is the target of the operation) as an argument of `_getsizing` is an oversight, which as done by you can be corrected by having `data=None` as a default argument.

It's not about *respecting* the platform. It is open to play, experiment, modify and adapt to what you need.

With regards to your questions:

  - Yes, the `sizer` operates as if you had calculated the stakes manually in the `strategy`. It is just an abstraction concept to encapsulate logic in a reusable manner.

  - The `broker` is a mere order taker and executor.

  - Yes, it you place an order with all your cash and the price raises your order will not be accepted (the 1st step is submission) if this is a "Market" or `Close`order.

  - The only way effectively control the price (and therefore the cash) is to use `Limit` orders (the broker has logic to try to give you a better price if possible by checking the ohlc prices to see if the limit can be improved)

The real question in the background seems to be the actual execution point of the order. First an axiom which is part of the design:

  - When the strategy's `next` method is called, the strategy evaluates a bar of prices. A bar which is already "closed", i.e: it has already happened. It's the past and the prices contained in the `high` - `low` range may not be seen again ever.

With that in mind the broker operates as follows for the order types:

  - `Market`

    The opening price of the next bar is used. The issues wants to get matched at the next tick and the next new tick is the next opening price.

  - `Close`

    This order type is meant for intraday backtesting. If the current development of the day events, then the order will be executed with the last "tick" of the session: the `close` price of the session. Still the order will first executed with the next bar. Of course if the order is when the last bar of day `x` is seen, the order will be executed with the last tick of day `x + 1`

  - `Limit`

    The order will be executed starting with the next bar if the limit price is seen. In some cases a better price will be attained, like if the `open` price is below the specified limit.
  
In your case

  - Your strategy`s `next` method is called at bar `x`

  - You issue an order for the entire amount of your cash.

  - The `broker` takes it and marks it as submitted.

  - The system fetches bar `x + 1`

  - The broker checks if the order can be `accepted` (enough cash) and if not issues a `margin` call and rejects the order

  - Order execution takes place (if possible and depending on type order)

  - Your strategy's `next` method is called again and you can evaluate the happenings at point `x + 1`

With this in mind it should be clear why your order is not being executed: not enough cash. (whether it is sensible to use all the cash is a topic for another discussion)

Is it unrealistic what you want? I cannot really answer this question.

But I can answer a similar one: is it realistic what `backtrader` does? That fully depends on the expectations of the end user. The real world is made of an unlimited number of cores and threads executing simultaneously and producing events (ticks, orders ...) and the platform operates with "past" data.

For me, the author, it is enough and I am happy with recreating a day via `replay` from `1 minute` bars. It's not the same thing I saw when looking at the live development of the chart but it is enough for me for all intents and purposes.

An analogy would be that of digital and analog recordings. The digital ones make a compromise to represent the analog reality in terms of sampling bits and sampling frequency. Some information is lost and even more when compressed to things like mp3, but the result is good enough when played back even it is not exactly the same as the original.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
# -*- coding: utf-8; py-indent-offset:4 -*-
from __future__ import (absolute_import, division, print_function,
                        unicode_literals)


import argparse
import datetime

import backtrader as bt


class St(bt.Strategy):
    params = dict(
        ma=bt.ind.SMA,
        p1=5,
        p2=15,
        limit=0.005,
        limdays=3,
        limdays2=1000,
        hold=10,
        usetarget=False,  # use order_target_size
        switchp1p2=False,  # switch prices of order1 and order2
        oco1oco2=False,  # False - use order1 as oco for order3, else order2
        do_oco=True,  # use oco or not
    )

    def notify_order(self, order):
        print('{}: Order ref: {} / Type {} / Status {}'.format(
            self.data.datetime.date(0),
            order.ref, 'Buy' * order.isbuy() or 'Sell',
            order.getstatusname()))

        if order.status == order.Completed:
            self.holdstart = len(self)

        if not order.alive() and order.ref in self.orefs:
            self.orefs.remove(order.ref)

    def __init__(self):
        ma1, ma2 = self.p.ma(period=self.p.p1), self.p.ma(period=self.p.p2)
        self.cross = bt.ind.CrossOver(ma1, ma2)

        self.orefs = list()

        if self.p.usetarget:
            print('-' * 5, 'Using order_target_size')
            self._dobuy = self.order_target_size
            self._doclose = self.order_target_size
        else:
            self._dobuy = self.buy
            self._doclose = self.close

    def next(self):
        if self.orefs:
            return  # pending orders do nothing

        if not self.position:
            if self.cross > 0.0:  # crossing up

                p1 = self.data.close[0] * (1.0 - self.p.limit)
                p2 = self.data.close[0] * (1.0 - 2 * 2 * self.p.limit)
                p3 = self.data.close[0] * (1.0 - 3 * 3 * self.p.limit)

                valid1 = datetime.timedelta(self.p.limdays)
                valid2 = valid3 = datetime.timedelta(self.p.limdays2)

                if self.p.switchp1p2:
                    p1, p2 = p2, p1
                    valid1, valid2 = valid2, valid1

                print('valid1 is:', valid1)

                kargs = dict(exectype=bt.Order.Limit)
                kargs[('target' * self.p.usetarget) or 'size'] = 1

                o1 = self._dobuy(price=p1, valid=valid1, **kargs)
                print('{}: Oref {} / Buy at {}'.format(
                    self.datetime.date(), o1.ref, p1))

                oco2 = o1 if self.p.do_oco else None
                o2 = self._dobuy(price=p2, valid=valid2, oco=oco2, **kargs)

                print('{}: Oref {} / Buy at {}'.format(
                    self.datetime.date(), o2.ref, p2))

                if self.p.do_oco:
                    oco3 = o1 if not self.p.oco1oco2 else oco2
                else:
                    oco3 = None

                o3 = self._dobuy(price=p3, valid=valid3, oco=oco3, **kargs)

                print('{}: Oref {} / Buy at {}'.format(
                    self.datetime.date(), o3.ref, p3))

                self.orefs = [o1.ref, o2.ref, o3.ref]

        else:  # in the market
            if (len(self) - self.holdstart) >= self.p.hold:
                self._doclose()


def runstrat(args=None):
    args = parse_args(args)

    cerebro = bt.Cerebro()

    # Data feed kwargs
    kwargs = dict()

    # Parse from/to-date
    dtfmt, tmfmt = '%Y-%m-%d', 'T%H:%M:%S'
    for a, d in ((getattr(args, x), x) for x in ['fromdate', 'todate']):
        if a:
            strpfmt = dtfmt + tmfmt * ('T' in a)
            kwargs[d] = datetime.datetime.strptime(a, strpfmt)

    # Data feed
    data0 = bt.feeds.BacktraderCSVData(dataname=args.data0, **kwargs)
    cerebro.adddata(data0)

    # Broker
    cerebro.broker = bt.brokers.BackBroker(**eval('dict(' + args.broker + ')'))

    # Sizer
    cerebro.addsizer(bt.sizers.FixedSize, **eval('dict(' + args.sizer + ')'))

    # Strategy
    cerebro.addstrategy(St, **eval('dict(' + args.strat + ')'))

    # Execute
    cerebro.run(**eval('dict(' + args.cerebro + ')'))

    if args.plot:  # Plot if requested to
        cerebro.plot(**eval('dict(' + args.plot + ')'))


def parse_args(pargs=None):
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
        formatter_class=argparse.ArgumentDefaultsHelpFormatter,
        description=(
            'Sample Skeleton'
        )
    )

    parser.add_argument('--data0', default='../../datas/2005-2006-day-001.txt',
                        required=False, help='Data to read in')

    # Defaults for dates
    parser.add_argument('--fromdate', required=False, default='',
                        help='Date[time] in YYYY-MM-DD[THH:MM:SS] format')

    parser.add_argument('--todate', required=False, default='',
                        help='Date[time] in YYYY-MM-DD[THH:MM:SS] format')

    parser.add_argument('--cerebro', required=False, default='',
                        metavar='kwargs', help='kwargs in key=value format')

    parser.add_argument('--broker', required=False, default='',
                        metavar='kwargs', help='kwargs in key=value format')

    parser.add_argument('--sizer', required=False, default='',
                        metavar='kwargs', help='kwargs in key=value format')

    parser.add_argument('--strat', required=False, default='',
                        metavar='kwargs', help='kwargs in key=value format')

    parser.add_argument('--plot', required=False, default='',
                        nargs='?', const='{}',
                        metavar='kwargs', help='kwargs in key=value format')

    return parser.parse_args(pargs)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    runstrat()
```

Ok, from what I've discovered there's essentially nothing wrong with PyHum. I have processed R0004 and R0005 from the example data sets made available to me. The following settings work well (note that, for reasons described before, model=1999 is correct):

```
   # reading specific settings
   cs2cs_args = "epsg:26791"
   bedpick = 1 # auto bed pick
   c = 1450 # speed of sound fresh water
   t = 0.108 # length of transducer
   draft = 0.3 # draft in metres
   flip_lr = 1 # flip port and starboard
   model = 1199 # humminbird model
   cog = 1 # GPS course-over-ground used for heading
   calc_bearing = 1 #0=no, 1=yes
   filt_bearing = 0 #0=no, 1=yes
   chunk = 'd1000' # distance, 1000m
```

I have set the 'calc_bearing' flag to 1, because recalculating headings works better in your case. I have zipped up all my processing files which are available here:

ftp://ftpext.usgs.gov/pub/wr/az/flagstaff/dbuscombe/Debbout_R00004.zip

and here:

ftp://ftpext.usgs.gov/pub/wr/az/flagstaff/dbuscombe/Debbout_R00005.zip

Before you download, note these are **really big files.** 

You should notice that the scans sometimes didn't georectify well. I believe this is the result of using a GPS input only every 3 seconds. This is rather slow (much slower than a 'native' Humminbird GPS receiver which has worked quite well for me) and may not capture changes in heading. If, as you say, the scans map correctly using SonarTRX, then I wish I knew the magic trick being used there. I will investigate further, seeing if I can use this data as a case study for how to fix bad georectifications. If I make any progress, I will let you know. I used 'd1000' for chunk argument (1 km long chunks) but maybe smaller, say 200m chunk lengths, would give you more usable data 

Also, there are gaps in the sonar coverage. That is because you tended to conduct your surveys rather too fast (> 2 m/s for most of the survey in R00005, for example). Given the pulse length and coverage, in your case <1.5 m/s would have ensured greater coverage. Of course, I realise that may not have been possible. If you don't see these gaps using sonarTRX, I suggest that that program is merely 'interpolating' across the gaps. There are ways to do that in PyHum too, or an external program

Anyway, sorry for the delay. I hope that helps.

Dan

awslogs simply uses the profile configured in your AWS config files, so the following will work provided the role you are assuming allows you access to CloudWatch.
 
Step 1: Assume you have a profile configured named **default** in your $HOME/.aws/credentials
Step 2: Add the role you are assuming to your $HOME/.aws/config, like this replacing 123456789012 with a valid accountId and ROLE_NAME with the role you are assuming
```shell
[profile someName]
role_arn = arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/ROLE_NAME
source_profile = default
region = us-east-1
```
Step 3: `awslogs groups --profile someName`

Hehe. LOL well Exhale is reliably slower [for different reasons](http://exhale.readthedocs.io/en/latest/faq.html#why-does-my-documentation-take-so-long-to-build).

Here's a braindump.  It is probably more information than you want...

I'm finishing up some touches to upload it to `pip` (finally, initial upload had a small bug and apparently I **did** get templates working).

### Worth Keeping in Mind

The "legacy" exhale was done in one file, and (through dubious `__dict__` crawling of `breathe`) explicitly crawled the `breathe` API to infer relationships.  In the rewrite, I switched to BeautifulSoup with an `lxml` backend.  The reason I bring this up is because I'm pretty sure the only reason "legacy" exhale even worked is because **`breathe` loads the ENTIRE XML DOM into memory**.  Don't quote me on that because I really have no idea.  I was never able to figure out how to extract the actual documentation through`breathe` compounds, so this could also indicate that the entire XML DOM is **not** loaded into memory.

### Notes on BeautifulSoup and lxml

1. BeautifulSoup for parsing because I'm lazy and its crazy convenient.  It allows me to modify the parsed XML *in place*, which was really useful for extracting file level documentation.  I can't use the breathe file directive because it will generate hundreds of duplicate ids.
    - This should not apply to `breathe`, because the generated API was constructed to match the Doxygen hierarchy.  AKA the parsing shouldn't need to change.
    - Trying to switch to BeautifulSoup for `breathe` is an exercise in futility, as the generated super stuff is already in play.
        - In the Exhale rewrite, I literally poured over XML document after XML document and basically just had to decide what portions of the XML document I needed to care about were.  `breathe` already has the full XML specs represented.
2. An undocumented but reliable feature of RTD is that `lxml` **is already installed**.  It does add some potential incompatibility (for Windows in particular) when doing `pip install breathe`, but you can also fallback on `minidom` if not present.
    - I don't know why it's installed, but I think it is safe to assume it will always be there.
    - For `breathe`, `setup.py` basically would need to `try` - `except` importing `lxml` **before** specifying the requirements for installation.  I don't think there is a way to try installing `lxml` and then fallback though, which leaves people vulnerable to having to **first** `pip install lxml` and then `pip install breathe`.  Presumably most people will overlook that, even if its documented.
    - Correspondingly, the parser would do the same thing but in the `except` just import the existing `minidom` or whatever.

### Parsing the API and Memory

In Exhale, I was very cautious to **not** build out the entire graph **while loading in all XML documents**.  I know for a fact that I can get away with this, but I'm not sure that `breathe` can.  The general workflow was

1. Parse all of `index.xml` and start creating nodes to represent the graph.
2. After `index.xml` is closed, individual (e.g., class or namespace or file) XML documents are opened, parsed, and closed.
    - I do have a future TODO because for files I **do** save the entire XML `programlisting`.  That needs to go away at some point.

So basically, the question for `breathe` really is: **does the ENTIRE XML DOM get loaded into memory at once**?  The above approach works because `index.xml` enumerates **every** possible type in the whole framework.  Then for things where `<refid>.xml` exists, you can use that to get more information such as base or derived classes, documentation of specific `refid`'s etc.

### In the end

I think I'm ready to re-upload to `pip` tonight, I'll post back when I have because Exhale prints out the times it takes to parse and generate the rst docs, so it may be useful for you to compare that to breathe times.  To test the breathe timing, though, you'd need to be working with a [Development Install](https://packaging.python.org/tutorials/distributing-packages/#working-in-development-mode) and inject some of your own benchmarks.

**Note**: I don't actually have any memory benchmarks for Exhale, only timing.  I only know how to do memory benchmarks in C++...

breathe_domain_by_extension = {"h" : "cpp"}
```

I'm somewhat familiar with the situation. `pip-review` cannot be merged into `pip` 

because `pip` does not have a dependency solver,
because implementing a dependency solver in `pip` is currently impossible,
because the `pip` maintainers will not accept any solutions until some PEP is completed, and it's not
because the PEP they want completed is currently "on hold"
because they want another PEP to be completed first, which is also "on hold"
(at this point I just gave up on figuring out the traceback)

Now, it would actually be possible to implement a dependency solver into `pip-review` because enthought has already developed the algorithm. But the questions are 

1. Would @jgonggrijp be okay with that?
2. Would @jgonggrijp, @xoviat, or someone else have time to do this?
3. Is this even possible? Well...no. Because it turns out that @nlhkabu tried to allow requirements to be downloaded from pypi, but failed because...wait for it...they want another PEP to be completed first.

So no, I wouldn't bet on integrating this into `pip`. Or `pip` resolving these issues anytime soon. The reason that I use this project is that I have given up on `pip`.

Regarding to your suggestions:

> I would like to include a method in Surface and Curve to return the output of deriv_ctrlpts

I guess this is also possible. However, this kind of change will require a little bit more than you may anticipate, for instance dealing the with the abstract methods. Let me explain what I was thinking while working on the evaluators:

An evaluator should provide the basic evaluation functionality, such as single and multiple knot point and derivative evaluation (you may consider checking out `Abstact.Evaluator` class). Therefore, a user who wants to implement a completely new evaluator with different algorithms (I believe other spline algorithms could fit here) is asked to implement 4 methods in his/her new evaluator class. On the other hand, if any more advanced algorithms or some custom functionalities are required, then the user may create a second class (e.g. `Abstract.CurveEvaluator` or `Abstract.SurfaceEvaluator` classes) and use multiple inheritance to extend his/her evaluator with the new functionality. This is the case for the Knot Insertion algorithms in curves and surfaces.

For the *derivatives of the control points* case, what I would recommend is first adding the method (e.g. `derivatives_ctrlpts`) to `Abstract.CurveEvaluator` or `Abstract.SurfaceEvaluator` classes (**not** to the `Abstract.Evaluator` class). Even though I added `staticmethod` with the latest commits, it looks like it is not required at all, then the decorator can be removed completely. The new method should be added to the main evaluator implementations, `evaluators.CurveEvaluator` and `evaluators.SurfaceEvaluator` and therefore, it will be inherited by `evaluators.CurveEvaluator2` and `evaluators.SurfaceEvaluator2` by default.

After these implementations, a wrapper method may be added to `BSpline.Curve` and `BSpline.Surface` classes and it should be tested with the `NURBS` module as well.

It looks like a lot of work to do but written explanation takes more time than doing it. If it is too confusing for you, I can take care of this part pretty quickly, since we have all the algorithms right now.

> I think default values for r1, r2, s1, s2 in deriv_ctrlpts should be the number of control points so that all control points of the derivated curve / surface are returned when the function is called without these arguments

I think this is acceptable. If it really works in that way with the default values you are providing, then I would not be against the change. Making the things a little bit easier is always accepted.

For the default values, I would suggest putting them inside the wrapper function of the `BSpline.Curve` and `BSpline.Surface` classes as the default value of `kwargs.get` function just to make it a little bit more convenient.

I think there are some features (like slots) that should be exposed directly since they're unique to Python and don't correspond with any general characteristics of data type definitions, but I do think there are others (like orderable and hashable) where a higher level formulation would make sense.

The latter would only be appropriate in cases where such a higher level formulation already appears somewhere in the standard library, as is the case with `collections.abc.Hashable` and `functools.total_ordering`.

A couple of other characteristics that may fail into that "higher level" category would be to support copying and pickling objects by default and then have a boolean toggle to turn that off at the class level:

- `copy=True`: delegate copy & deepcopy support to the interpreter based on the defined fields, without getting into protocol details (complains if a defined field can't be copied & isn't excluded from copying)
- `pickle=True`: delegate pickle support to the interpreter based on the defined fields, without getting into protocol details (complains if a defined field can't be pickled & isn't excluded from pickling)

I don't think you'd ever call the base class `__init__`, although you might call `__post_init__`. You wouldn't call the base class `__init__` because all of the fields have already been initialized in the derived class's `__init__`. I'll see about an example with `__post_init__` chaining.

Hi @hargup,

Are you familiar with the GNU `parallel` command-line tool?: https://www.gnu.org/software/parallel

This is what I usually use to parallelize uploads (as well as for other `ia` functions).

One thing to note with uploads, regardless of how you decide to submit concurrent uploads, is that it does not really pay to upload to a single item concurrently. It is recommended to upload to different items in parallel.

A basic example might be something like: Let's say you have a list of identifiers you would like to create/upload to in a file called itemlist.txt:

    my_id1
    my_id2
    my_id3

With files named like `<identifier>.pdf`. Then, you could use parallel like so:

    parallel 'ia upload {} {}.pdf -m collection:my_collection -m mediatype:texts --retries 30' < itemlist.txt

I usually do something like this, providing minimal metadata that is the same across all items (e.g. collection, mediatype), and then add unique metadata after the upload has completed (i.e. `ia metadata --spreadsheet=metadata.csv`).

Does this make sense? Please feel free to email me if you have more questions that are specific to your use case, otherwise followup here.

Sure

So at a high level this is how diff cover works.

Using the subprocess module diff-cover runs git commands to get the diff and find out information the project.

By parsing that output and comparing it to the output of a quality command like pep8 or a coverage report it can give the status of the lines that changed by ignoring the lines that have not.

It relies on the following commands.

`git rev-parse --show-toplevel --encoding=utf-8`
`git diff <branch>..head --no-ext-diff`
`git diff --no-ext-diff`
`git diff --cached --no-ext-diff`

So to create a way to do this with merc you would have to figure out rough equivelents of hese commands and be able to parse the output of those commands ( you might save time with the --git flag when running `hg diff`)

At a code level you would need hg versions of the `GitDiffReporter` in `diff_reporter.py` some a hg version of the `GitPathTool` in `git_path.py` (or generalize it to work with hg and git) and an hg version of the `GitDiffTool`

Then I think you just need an -hg switch in the commands themselves and read that to decide what instances of these objects to use.

If you think you are up for that let me know. Obviously I can support.

If you don't think you will be able to thats totally ok :-) 

At the very least, do you know what the equivalent versions of those git commands would be in hg? Im not even sure hg has the same `staging` idea that git does.

Here is what I came up with

`git rev-parse --show-toplevel --encoding=utf-8` - > `hg root`
`git diff <branch>..head --no-ext-diff` -> `hg diff -r "ancestor(<branch>, .)"`
`git diff --no-ext-diff` ->  `hg diff --git`
`git diff --cached --no-ext-diff` -> ?

But I have not used hg very much.

Pylint needs to be used on a Python package or module. "ignore" in .pylintrc can be used to skip something in that selected context. If diff-quality runs on every file by itself then cutting out a module is unfeasible. But I'm no expert, I'm just telling you what I've worked out :)

I've also tried running diff-quality only in the folder that I want checked... It doesn't seem to detect any problems then.

Anyway, I guess I'll be using my custom script to do the checking, so you can close the issue if you want. Or you can leave this as some feature request :)

`loaded_fields` is actually necessary.

At [this point](https://github.com/aykut/django-bulk-update/blob/master/django_bulk_update/helper.py#L136) `fields` can be `None` or an **empty list**. This difference is important.

- If `fields` is an empty list we exit the `bulk_update`, as you said.
- But if `fields` is `None` we go ahead. This happens when we don't have `meta` nor `update_fields`. In this case we don't know which fields we have to update, and since each object could have a different set of loaded variables, we have to call `get_fields` for each one.

For reference for any other package maintainers that run into this:

My development setup is Ubuntu 18.04:
```
$ cat /etc/lsb-release 
DISTRIB_ID=Ubuntu
DISTRIB_RELEASE=18.04
DISTRIB_CODENAME=bionic
DISTRIB_DESCRIPTION="Ubuntu 18.04 LTS"
```
and python 3.6 and 3.7 come packaged:
```
$ ls /usr/bin/python[0-9].[0-9]
/usr/bin/python2.7  /usr/bin/python3.6  /usr/bin/python3.7
```
but only one version of Cython for python3:
```
$ ls /usr/bin/cython*
/usr/bin/cython  /usr/bin/cython3
```
So to get the latest version of cython with the fix, I installed it in virtualenv and used that:
```
$ virtualenv -p python3.7 env37
$ env37/bin/pip install cython
$ rm accumulation_tree/accumulation_tree.c # force rebuild
$ env/bin/python setup.py build # builds it with the new cython
```
now, you can just use whatever python version to test that it builds, and use `setup.py sdist` to create a new tarball. Use `twine` to upload it to PyPI.

Hope that helps someone!

Question A:

When you get face feature landmarks for a face, you can check the location of points that should be parallel (like the outer edge of both eyes or tops of both sides of chin) and then take the angle between those points to get the angle of the face in the image.

Question B: 

The same as A - use the detected face landmarks to resize the face to a pre-determined size. Like if the two sides of the chin are 200 pixels apart but you want a face 150 pixels wide, shrink the image down to match.

OR you can do both A and B in one step: Detect all the face landmarks in your image and then apply a transformation to make the face landmarks in your image match a pre-defined template. This is usually called "face alignment". This library does that internally in dlib's C code, but you can do it manually in Python code.

For example, here's how OpenFace does it in Python code using an affine transformation to make a detected face transform to match a pre-determined face landmark map: https://github.com/cmusatyalab/openface/blob/71ee6ee7477be8fe2a2865b5b60fdfef52cf5783/openface/align_dlib.py#L182-L187 (Note that their approach DOES distort the face somewhat which you might not want)

And here's OpenFace's pre-determined landmark map they are warping each face to match: https://github.com/cmusatyalab/openface/blob/71ee6ee7477be8fe2a2865b5b60fdfef52cf5783/openface/align_dlib.py#L22-L59 - each of those points maps to one face landmark point

Ok, I started already because I need it urgently. I've found that `_meta.many_to_many` is not intended for usage, they recommend `get_fields`:

https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.8/_modules/django/db/models/options/
```
    @cached_property
    def many_to_many(self):
        """
        Returns a list of all many to many fields on the model and its parents.

        Private API intended only to be used by Django itself; get_fields()
        combined with filtering of field properties is the public API for
        obtaining this list.
        """
        return make_immutable_fields_list(
            "many_to_many",
            (f for f in self._get_fields(reverse=False)
            if f.is_relation and f.many_to_many)
        )
```

'reverse=False' above means that it's intended only for relations defined on this model.

from lifelines.datasets import load_dd

democracy_1 = data.loc[data['democracy'] == 'Democracy']

If like me you are not married to getting that auth token then you may follow this route using cookies instead:

```python
csrf_req = requests.get(url + '/login_csrf')
csrf_token = csrf_req.json()['csrf_token']

login_req = requests.post(url + '/login',
    data={ 
        'email': email, 
        'password': password,
        'csrf_token': csrf_token
    },
    cookies=csrf_req.cookies,
    allow_redirects=False
)
```

Finally, you can then do:

```python
cookies = login_req.cookies
requests.get(url, cookies=cookies)
```

You may use the cookies for further requests. If you don't want to setup that `/login_csrf` route you can use `BeautifulSoup` too:

```python
csrf_req = requests.get(url + '/login', allow_redirects=False)

In response to your proposal, I'll add the factory class named ``testing.postgresql.PostgresqlFactory``.
For example:
```
import unittest
import testing.postgresql

# Generate Postgresql class which caches the generated database
Postgresql = testing.postgresql.PostgresqlFactory(use_initdb_cache=True)


def tearDownModule(self):
    # clear cached database at end of tests
    Postgresql.clear_cache()


class MyTestCase(unittest.TestCase):
    def setUp(self):
        # Use the generated Postgresql class instead of testing.postgresql.Postgresql
        self.postgresql = Postgresql()

    def tearDown(self):
        self.postgresql.stop()
```
It makes testcases more efficient.

The factory class supports all options of `testing.postgresql.Postgresql`.
So, you can use `copy_data_from` option:
```
Postgresql = testing.postgresql.PostgresqlFactory(copy_data_from='/path/to/your/appdir`)
with Postgresql() as pgsql:
    # ...
```

It's been a while, but to those who're seeking to use this.

you can create your own `process_request` method and building the logic to assign `request.user`, one example of decoding jwt would be:

```
def process_request(request):
	request.user = None
	token = request.REQUEST.get('token', None)
	if token:
		try:
			payload = jwt_decode_handler(token)
		except jwt.ExpiredSignature:
			msg = 'Signature has expired.'
			raise exceptions.AuthenticationFailed(msg)
		except jwt.DecodeError:
			msg = 'Error decoding signature.'
			raise exceptions.AuthenticationFailed(msg)
		try:
			user_id = jwt_get_user_id_from_payload(payload)
			if user_id:
				request.user = User.objects.get(pk=user_id, is_active=True)
			else:
				msg = 'Invalid payload'
				raise exceptions.AuthenticationFailed(msg)
		except User.DoesNotExist:
			msg = 'Invalid signature'
			raise exceptions.AuthenticationFailed(msg)
```

then of course in your settings, tell ws4redis to use this custom function
```
from <your package> import process_request
WS4REDIS_PROCESS_REQUEST = process_request
```

Quick workaround I used just now that specifically fixes this for Django Import/Export:

1. Ensure DjangoObjectActions is before ExportMixin in your ModelAdmin class declaration
2. Add 'redirect_to_export' to your changelist_actions
3. Add the following method to your ModelAdmin

    def redirect_to_export(self, request, obj):
        return HttpResponseRedirect(reverse('admin:%s_%s_export' % self.get_model_info()))
    redirect_to_export.label = "Export"

This works for Export but it should be clear how to do the same for import

You can already pass in ``Path`` objects to ``INotify.add_watch()``, since ``Path`` objects have an ``encode()`` method returning ``bytes`` - which is all that `INotify.add_watch()`` cares about. So that already works for anyone using ``Path`` objects.

The only other place a change would occur is in the ``Event`` objects - they would be changed to contain a ``Path`` object for the name instead of a string. I don't think this is the correct behaviour because the names coming from inotify are not actually paths - they're just a single name of an entry in a directory being watched. So constructing a full path would mean we would no longer be a literal wrapper of inotify. 

In any case, ceating a full filepath would require knowing all the parent directories, which might not even be unambiguously knowable due to the existence of links, and would be difficult to construct due to race conditions. So any attempt at this would not live up to the 'simple' moniker in the package name.

But as I said, it's not the right behaviour anyway. The only non-racey way to know that name's parent directory is the watch descriptor that the event comes in on. Using full filepaths isn't the right approach when inotify is all about the fact that these things can change at a moment's notice. To get correct filepaths (where 'correct' means 'correct at the time of the last inotify event we processed') you would have to maintain all the filepaths in some datastructure, be watching every parent directory and responding to move events by recursively updating all the paths in the datastructure. It's tricky! And whilst that's one *application* of inotify, it's not what inotify itself does and so doesn't belong in this package.

Work is almost done on this.  Moving forward, custom fences will be generalized.  You will be able to feed in a list of custom fences with the following as the default.

```py
[
    {'name': 'flow', 'class': 'uml-flowchart', 'format': superfences.fence_code_format},
    {'name': 'sequence', 'class': 'uml-sequence-diagram', 'format': superfences.fence_code_format}
]
```

You will specify the fence langauge name, the class to apply to the HTML element that is output, and the format function that outputs the HTML.  The format function will take the source, fence name, and HTML class.  Two format functions will be provided with SuperFences: `fence_code_format` which will wrap the source in pre/code blocks, and `fence_div_format` which will wrap the source in `div`.  If desired custom format functions can be written to different or more complex outputs.

In any case, I think a 100% solution would be to compile a query on validation, which unions the data from the table with the record in memory like this:

```sql
WITH existing_records AS (
    SELECT "group"
    FROM job
    WHERE is_complete = False 
    -- AND pk != <saved_object_pk>

    UNION ALL

    SELECT "group"
    FROM (
        SELECT <saved_object_pk> AS id, 'A' AS "group", True AS "is_complete"
    ) AS record_to_save
    WHERE is_complete = False
)
SELECT COUNT(*) AS count
FROM existing_records
```

Then check in Python whether `count <= 1`. This allows us to just dump the `WHERE` from the partial index into both parts of the query. The hard part is generating the UNION and the in-memory subselect that is generated with the values of the current object. After some investigation, I think I understand where the problem is coming from.

In the current setup of operators, it is not possible to save a state inside of the operators such as pid or in our case, the id of a spark job.
The reason is that there is a crucial difference between the saved task (the operator) and a job (a TaskInstance).

When you clear a job, it will clear the TaskInstances and not the tasks (and therefore has no previous state which you saved in the operator.

How I see a possible solution is a mechanism which you can use inside of an operator to specify what kind of information you want to save. And this state can be accessed from a TaskInstance.

@plypaul via https://cwiki.apache.org/confluence/display/AIRFLOW/Scheduler+Basics

> The scheduler processes tasks that have a state of NONE, QUEUED, and UP_FOR_RETRY. NONE is a newly created TaskInstance, QUEUED is a task that is waiting for a slot in an executor and UP_FOR_RETRY means a task that failed before but needs to be retried. At the moment the scheduler will not change the state of a Task. This creates the possibility of a race condition: if the executor cannot execute a task quickly enough its state will not be updated and it can get scheduled again. Due to the loop times being quite large in complex cases, this won't occur too often. As people try to limit scheduler loop times, or just prefer LocalExecutor more than the CeleryExecutor, they will run multiple schedulers at the same time, which increases the chances of this happening.
>
> For this reason a "SCHEDULED" state is proposed in one of the PRs. This will not fully close the door to this race condition due to the fact that TaskInstance evaluate their own state in case of UP_FOR_RETRY (ie. checking if they should run now). Normally one would move this check to the scheduler was it not for that fact that we have backfills...

Basically, without a SCHEDULED state, the task is given to the executor. If the scheduler loops around fast enough again before the executor updates the task's state, the scheduler will schedule it *again*, since the task's state hasn't changed (and it's still runnable).

Is the libzmq.so included in the bundle, just not loaded?

The main benefit is to be able to integrate with various libraries that deal with user input such as CLI toolkits, config parsers, ORM etc. `urlparse` is too abstract to be useful as it can only represent 2 components of a zeromq endpoint: scheme and netloc. In my development experience I often need to access just a port or just an interface of a TCP endpoint. And there are other, more complex transports.

Although it can be developed as an independent package, having it bundled provide the following benefits:

- Native support by pyzmq methods, so one can pass an object into bind / connect etc (implementation only needs to wrap arguments with `str` or `repr`)
- Easier to keep it in sync with zeromq API
- More attention would allow to have one "definitive" implementation

unbind will raise this error if the given socket is not bound on the given URL, such as if you call `unbind` with the wrong port or more than once:

```python
import zmq
ctx = zmq.Context()
s = ctx.socket(zmq.PUB)
url = 'tcp://127.0.0.1:5555'
s.bind(url)

s.unbind(url) # ok
s.unbind(url) # raises No such file...
```

Hi! This is something that comes up from time-to-time and while I understand the crux of the issue, I really don't want to do this.

Bleach is a security-focused library. The problem domain is HUGE. It's really important to keep the complexity as minimal as possible so as to ease the maintenance burden and also to prevent security issues.

Not accepting this shouldn't block you or anyone else from doing things you need to do. Bleach is flexible and you can use your own allowed-tags list. I don't think this is a must-have feature.

If you want to start a project with a list of non-minimal tags that you assert are safe, that'd be cool. Maybe there are like-minded people who would work with you on that.

Hope that helps!

Some other things that occurred to me:

1. For users who run the Vagrant environment from a Windows host, trying to create symlinks within the guest OS in a directory that is mapped to the host (ie virtualbox shared folder) causes errors without some workarounds on the host side. In the past I seem to remember errors trying to npm install certain packages in the VM since they create symlinks in node_modules. 
  As such, we'll need to check if this occurs with the new package.json packages and figure out a workaround/documentation if so (at least two developers working on Treeherder use Windows environments for the host, one of which is myself).

2) neutrino-preset-web uses babel-preset-env to determine which browsers to target, and by default uses `last 2 {Chrome,Firefox,Edge,Opera,Safari,iOS} versions` ([here](https://github.com/mozilla-neutrino/neutrino-dev/blob/f5712521246c0bdf6e27d5f3abfd3f7625030e66/packages/neutrino-preset-web/src/index.js#L129-L135)). Given that (a) Treeherder doesn't work well on mobile anyway, (b) it's a developer targeted tool, so we shouldn't need to support older browsers, I was wondering whether we should override and use "last 1 X version" instead, plus drop iOS/Safari from the list?
  Judging by [this caniuse.com result](http://caniuse.com/#compare=edge+13,edge+14,firefox+50,firefox+51,chrome+55,chrome+56,safari+9.1,safari+10,opera+42,opera+43,ios_saf+9.3,ios_saf+10.0-10.2&compare_cats=JS%20API) it would presumably mean much fewer babel plugins have to be used, speeding up build time and reducing bundle size bloat (eg older versions of Safari/iOS Safari don't even support arrow functions or `let`). Might be worth measuring to see.

@Tarrasch it's still true that `MyTask(**mydict).x == MyTask(**mydict).x` because the constructor will look these values up in the cache. This should be equivalent to setting the default like `DEFAULT_VALUE = datetime.datetime.now()` at the module level and reading that as your script default, it just gets set a little later. The only difference will be if you have multiple different instances of the same task using the default. So if `x` isn't in `dict1` or `dict2` you're no longer guaranteed that `MyTask(**dict1).x == MyTask(**dict2).x`.

I don't quite see the point in making this change given how easy it is to declare the default at the module level, but I can't see a downside either. where is `update_id` actually used?

>This seems like a cool addition; however, i'm not completely sold on it yet. It really feels like a specific wrapper around post_copy, but it's executed along side it.

You would be right to say that it's a wrapper around `post_copy` because in our original implementation, we were using `post_copy` instead of `post_copy_metacolumns`. 

I would agree that they are pretty much the same but we use `post_copy` as task-specifics post copy queries. For instance, we happen to use `post_copy` to deduplicate possible duplicated output. 

Alongside this, we also want to be able to run generic updates. For instance, `created_tz` timestamp. It made sense to decouple them, in our mind, to differentiate task-specific `post_copy` and generic updates to table with `post_copy_metacolumns`. 

I've created a gist that may better demonstrate how we use it: https://gist.github.com/cabouffard/73cfc798bef9ec09fc91807b99a6c5a9

That said, having this separated in two different methods, allow us to not have to override the content of `post_copy`  if we want to add generic metadata_columns behavior.

>The end goal you're trying to achieve is to easily modify a set series of metadata columns after performing a Redshift Copy. This feels like a finer-grained version of post_copy.

Here is the Code for Storage vmotion of the a VM.

'''
@auithor: sathvik.1991@gmail.com
'''

import atexit
import argparse
import sys
import time
import requests, ssl
from pyVmomi import vim, vmodl
from pyVim import connect
from pyVim.connect import Disconnect

inputs = {'vcenter_ip': 'x.x.x.x',
          'vcenter_password': 'Password',
          'vcenter_user': 'root',
          'vm_name': 'test-vm',
          'destination_host': 'x.x.x.x',
          'destination_datastore': "nas"
          }


def get_obj(content, vimtype, name):
    """
     Get the vsphere object associated with a given text name
    """
    obj = None
    container = content.viewManager.CreateContainerView(content.rootFolder, vimtype, True)
    for c in container.view:
        if c.name == name:
            obj = c
            break
    return obj


def wait_for_task(task, actionName='job', hideResult=False):
    """
    Waits and provides updates on a vSphere task
    """
    while task.info.state == vim.TaskInfo.State.running:
        time.sleep(2)
    if task.info.state == vim.TaskInfo.State.success:
        if task.info.result is not None and not hideResult:
            out = '%s completed successfully, result: %s' % (actionName, task.info.result)
            print out
        else:
            out = '%s completed successfully.' % actionName
            print out
    else:
        out = '%s did not complete successfully: %s' % (actionName, task.info.error)
        raise task.info.error
        print out

    return task.info.result


def main():
    # Disabling urllib3 ssl warnings
    requests.packages.urllib3.disable_warnings()

    # Disabling SSL certificate verification
    context = ssl.SSLContext(ssl.PROTOCOL_TLSv1)
    context.verify_mode = ssl.CERT_NONE
    try:
        si = None
        try:
            print "Trying to connect to VCENTER SERVER . . ."
            si = connect.Connect(inputs['vcenter_ip'], 443,
                    inputs['vcenter_user'], inputs['vcenter_password'],
                    sslContext=context, service="hostd")
        except IOError, e:
            pass
            atexit.register(Disconnect, si)

        print "Connected to VCENTER SERVER !"

        content = si.RetrieveContent()

        vm = get_obj(content, [vim.VirtualMachine], inputs['vm_name'])
        destination_host = get_obj(content, [vim.HostSystem], inputs['destination_host'])

        destination_datastore = get_obj(content,[vim.Datastore],
                destination_datastore)
        resource_pool = vm.resourcePool

        migrate_priority = vim.VirtualMachine.MovePriority.defaultPriority

        print "Migrating %s to destination host %s" % (inputs['vm_name'], inputs['destination_host'])
        relocate_spec = vim.vm.RelocateSpec(datastore= destination_datastore, host=destination_host)
        task = vm.Relocate(relocate_spec)
        wait_for_task(task, si)

    except vmodl.MethodFault, e:
        print "Caught vmodl fault: %s" % e.msg
        return 1
    except Exception, e:
        print "Caught exception: %s" % str(e)
        return 1

# Start program
if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

working theory:  I think the issue here is in setup.py, that the minilib load never happens because we have already imported paver.tasks from the non-minilib-version and it's cached.

Sequence:
1) We try and import paver.tasks
2) That import is successful, but importing six fails and throws an exception in tasks.py
3) Exception handler in setup.py runs which calls sys.path.insert(0, 'paver-minilib.zip')
4) import paver.tasks is called which we THINK should call into the minilib zipfile, but instead, uses the cached 'import paver.tasks' from before (the one that failed), and calls that again, and it again fails with a 'six' error.   We're never making it inside the zipfile

I think that the new thing that's not being handled is the fact that ImportError was designed to catch the situation where 'import paver.tasks' failed _because it doesn't exist_.  Now that it's failing _because of a new error inside it (six package doesn't exist)_, the code in setup.py isn't handling it.

To demo: This successfully loads the minilib (the lib itself and generation appears fine)
```
sys.path.insert(0, 'paver-minilib.zip')
import paver.tasks
```

----

So, I think the answer is to either somehow test if a module will exist without importing it (not sure that's possible) or to unload it (imp.reload() ?).   I'll see if I can cook up a sample that works.

content = b'.....'
repo.index.add(path='path/to/file', content=content)
```

@perrygeo I definitely agree that a point exactly on a tile boundary should count as intersecting both tiles. I think the problem with fixing that assumption is this [test case](https://github.com/mapbox/mercantile/blob/master/tests/test_cli.py#L139) which enforces:

```
$ echo "[2331,1185,12]" | mercantile shapes | mercantile tiles 12
[2331, 1185, 12]
```

If a point on a boundary intersects both tiles, shouldn't the boundary of the tile then intersect all adjacent tiles? The result should be the center tile and its 8 surrounding tiles..? Is the round-trip test case and assumption wrong?

Either way I think `tiles()` should reflect the desired output and the CLI should just pass the values through. Otherwise the CLI and the library will have different behavior on identical inputs. Also, the padding is bound to cause trouble on some edge cases at scale. If `tiles()` had an explicit check, then the padding can be removed entirely - so I think the fix should be in `tiles()`, not the CLI.

In short this is handled by setuptools and it seems odd to call the packaging standard a bad practice but you should raise that issue there. We don’t parse `setup.py` or `setup.cfg` but instead simply allow pip’s internals to do so. 

Setup files are for installing a package. As an end user if I want to install a thing you made, your setup file tells my package manager (setuptools) where to put scripts and what dependencies I need. Requirements files don’t do anything besides list dependencies, so relying on one for distributing your software doesn’t make a ton of sense. Whatever abstraction you choose for handling these things, this will still be the case.

I also think this is a shortcoming. E.g. PyCharm supports developing inside Docker, and so there is no need to use virtualenvs, `--system` is fine. However, I still want the project to be using Pipenv, for version locking, for colleagues to be able to use virtualenvs etc.

External dependencies can be instantiated by main function and injected into sub-apps via function arguments like this:
```python
def users_app(app, db):
    @app.route('/<int:user_id>')
    def as_user(request, user_id):
        username = db.username_from_id(user_id)
        return 'A User Named "{username}"'.format(username=username)

db = connect_to_db()
app = Klein()
with app.subroute('/users/'):
    users_app(app, db)

app.run('localhost', 8123)
```
This is almost the same as passing deps to sub-app classes via __init__ args in your example. But this way we don't need to write `__init__` to store them into `self`, we can just use them directly.

I possibly have figured out the problem. Confluence maintains a connection pool to the proxy, so the connections between the client and the proxy and between the proxy and the server are both alive after a first search request. However, the connection between the proxy and server get severed after a certain amount of time, but the client is not aware of it. When subsequent requests from the client try to reuse the connections, the client finds that the other part, the connection from the proxy and server, is lost.

Is there a way to cut the client-to-proxy connection when a proxy-to-server connection is lost? And possibly vice versa? I think this would make the connection clean.

Yes, this is still a problem with the latest version as of now (version 1.2.5).

To recapitulate:
- Google Appengine does not support background threads for frontend instances (the instance type you generally want to use)
- Starting (and not stopping) threads on GAE will make your request "hang" because it requires each thread started during the request to finish before it finishes

To use the library on GAE, you need to:
- manually install it, its dependencies (`requests` and `python-dateutil`) and the transitive dependencies of `python-dateutil` (`six`) - [vendoring](https://cloud.google.com/appengine/docs/python/tools/using-libraries-python-27) may be of some help
- you also need to include `requests-toolbelt` to make requests work (or activate the paid-only sockets feature) - see [Issuing HTTP(S) Requests](https://cloud.google.com/appengine/docs/python/issue-requests) - you may still see [some spurious warnings](https://github.com/sigmavirus24/requests-toolbelt/issues/163)
- after each request you need to call `.join()`, otherwise your request will hang. Ie:

```python
analytics.track(...
analytics.join()
```

A much better architecture for this library would be to make both the "queuing" and "fetching" part pluggable. In particular for GAE Python 2.7 you probably want to:

- use a push queue for queuing (with a dedicated worker pulling from the queue and forwarding the times)
- use the urlfetch service (or perhaps even the asynchronous urlfetch service)

@philastrophist The calculation in `calc_total_errors` assumes that `data` and `bkg_error` have the same units.  This is required to do the addition with `Quantity` objects and to ensure the output total `error` array has the same units as the input `data`.  Sorry that wasn't documented or checked within the code.  I submitted a fix in #537 to explicitly require this and document it.

For your specific example, you effectively need to calculate `error = np.sqrt(data * gain + bkg_err**2)`.  However, this will fail with your `Quantity` inputs because the first term has units of `u.electron`, but the second term has units of `u.electron**2`.  For calculating the Poisson variance, the `u.electron` units should be treated essentially as dimensionless (counts).  The `calc_total_errors` functions handles this by assuming ``data`` and ``bkg_error`` have the same units.  For your specific example, you can either strip the units (having ensured the equation works for your units), use the above equation, and then add the units back in, or do this:

```python
>>> source_variance = (data * gain).to("electron2", equivalencies=[(u.Unit("electron"), u.Unit("electron2"))])
>>> error = np.sqrt(source_variance + bkg_err**2)
```

which will give `error` in units of `u.electron`.
