Running the example multi-threaded on 6 cores is 12 times faster when using this PR:

```Python
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor
from multiprocessing import cpu_count

max_workers = cpu_count() // 2
print('max_workers =', max_workers)

def map_threaded(function, *iterables, max_workers=max_workers):
    with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers) as executor:
        return list(executor.map(function, *iterables))
    
def function(img):
    probabilistic_hough_line(img, threshold=10, line_length=5, line_gap=3, seed=1234)

%timeit map_threaded(function, [edges] * max_workers)
```

@RDaneelOlivav I re-read this thread today, and I have some further input.

A big part of what swarming helps do is figure out the optimal way to encode input data into SDRs in the first place. The only benefit you would get from having a swarming process that accepts raw SDRs is that you would be able to help identify the best global algorithm parameters for the spatial pooler and temporal pooler. 

I think trying to swarm over SDR input is not possible as swarming works today, so I'm going to close this issue.

So, let's put some more context in there.

In case we want to build a generic *cliquet* client, it is important to have the *cliquet* protocol defined somewhere, in order to know if the client we're using is actually compatible with the version of the protocol.

For instance, let's say we change the way we handle pagination, we would need the client to know beforehand that the old version isn't supported.

Currently, cliquet only exposes the version of the application (what is in the setup.py) and as such doesn't convey this protocol information.

So, ideally, I think we should display the two information: version of the app *and* version of the protocol, since they're two different things.

Matlab does not support the default compression in deepdish (and PyTables) `'blosc'`. Because of it's narrow support, I plan to change the default in the next version of deepdish. Until then, you can manually change it:

```python
dd.io.save('test.h5', data, compression='zlib')  # compression widely supported
dd.io.save('test.h5', data, compression=None)    # no compressoin
```
It's unfortunate, since `'blosc'` is by far faster than `'zlib'` and in most situations as compressive.

You misunderstand, what I mean is that tests which only test the interfaces between the individual parser components could very easily become invalided by changes, especially those which fundamentally change parser state management such as supporting streamed execution (at least some of the other approaches) or concurrent execution. The current tests are guaranteed to be correct because they simulate the actual parsing. So, I mean yes, the current tests are pretty large and hard to read for edge cases, but that's because they're behaviour-based integration tests rather than unit tests. So, if I were to delete the existing tests and replace them with all the equivalent unit tests, then I think I could easily end up in a situation where I either have to delete and re-implement most of the unit tests or they fail to test real parser execution, which is why I went with this approach for testing to begin with aside from that I thought this was clearer since it's fairly obvious what user-facing behaviour each test is testing.
To clarify, I'm not against adding all of the equivalent unit tests, but I didn't implement them as such originally because I expected a high amount of near-future churn on them. And, I wouldn't trust them without corresponding integration tests. --- *Comments from [Reviewable](https://reviewable.io:443/reviews/google/mobly/416)*

I disagree on one point: 
  `max_nb_cycles` is an implicit inference, since it may block the computation toward the past and thus doesn't allow some values to be computed.

For your last remark, I think of the obvious `is_permanent` for  `date_naissance` but it seems to be treated by [the proposal](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-core/issues/442).

My suggestion was to recognize that the need of extrapolating a value to the past or future without having it permanent may be a good option to replace  
````
base_function = requested_period_last_or_next_value
````

>As now, some Python modules are used as "import forwarders", letting other modules to import them in order to benefit from the imports they made... wow!


>Think of `from numpy import (x, y, z)`, or `import numpy as np`, whereas `numpy` does not host all its functions in its `__init__.py` :-).

Yep, and to do this, numpy actually does... [import forwarding](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/master/numpy/__init__.py#L185) 😄 .

>A good surface API would allow users to do `from openfisca_core import (Variable, EnumCol, periods)`. Constants like `ADD` could be hosted under `Variable`, etc, etc. That would allow users to copy-paste a block of imports in their model modules which would be small *and* satisfy good practices, linting tools and wouldn't oblige to use `# noqa`.


"Copy paste a block" is an expression that raises huge alerts for me. Given the big number of imports we use to write formulas, that'd be a big block.

I understand that `from x import *` is a bad **python** practise, but model is not _really_ a python file : it's an **openfisca model** file, basically a DSL. As @MattiSG mentionned, we could _even_ have dependency injection. So to me it's fine to claim that in our context some python good practises are not really relevant.

>And rename `formulas_toolbox` to `imports_for_model` to make it explicit, and more exact (it's targeted to `model` modules which contain formulas and input variables :-))

I would go for `model_api` (as what we offer is not just about formulas). It contains a promise which brings some value : this is all what you need to build your model files.

>In short, and given the constraints, at least I'd like to replace the indirection `openfisca_france.model.x` -> `openfisca_france.model.base` -> `openfisca_core.formulas_toolbox` by `openfisca_france.model.x` -> `openfisca_core.imports_for_model`

The only difficulty is that you need two things to write formulas : 
- The core objects/functions/classes offered by the `model_api` module discussed here
- The entities defined in each tax and benefit system. 

The only role of `openfisca_france.model.base` is to combine these two. We could replace it by two lines of import in each model fine, but once more time I'd arbitrate towards simplicity for the formula writers, and keep the `base` module (and maybe rename it to `france.model_api`.

The idea was that you can set MESSAGE_ID either on the handler (in which case it lands in `_extras` and is added to all messages through that handler), or on each individual message. With your patch, setting it on the message is not possible any more.

The issue with current code is that if *both* are specified, we get an error, because MESSAGE_ID would be specified twice. So this should be fixed, but I think the ability to specify MESSAGE_ID on the message should be retained. If both are present, the one from the message should have higher priority, and the one from the handler should be ignored.

I don't have the time at the moment to very closely read this, but I think there are a lot of fundamental flaws in this pull request.

For example, operations on the original request shouldn't be done via `response.request` if we have to do anything like that, it should not be a constant access via the response object because attribute access hurts in Python more than people realize. Admittedly, there isn't automated CI for this project, but that doesn't mean we can just break backwards compatibility randomly. I'm also confused as to why we're releasing the socket but then using `response.connection` (which if memory serves me right is the `urllib3.HTTPConnection` (which should have said socket). @honnibal: I'm not sure I understand what you are asking, can I get some clarification?  Here's what I think I'm hearing:
1) You want something like Torque/SLURM.
2) There may be a bug in pathos.pools.ParallelPool, for nodes=0.

So, to address (1): If you want to use Torque/SLURM, yes, you can do that.  There's `pyina`, which is another one of my packages.  It's due this year to be updated as python3 compatible and released... however the code from the git repository is stable.  There will be some changes before the release, but I don't think anything that breaks backward compatibility.  However, it's `pathos` that has `ssh-tunnel` capabilities, if you need that for connection purposes, so that's when you might have to mix `pyina` and `pathos` pools/pipes together.

For (2), I'm less clear what you are asking.  Can you provide a sample code to show the error you get, so I can try it?  I can build a `ParallelPool` with no local nodes. balthild 也许我不配做一个程序员，但我的心是有激情的和善良的。2007年前我还不知道有github这个平台，我不知道开源的shadowsocks是哪年搞得， 但是我第一次见到ss是很多很多年前了。08年，因为防火墙， 我风风火火的网站，一个支撑了我所有信仰和希望的平台，一个聚集某大国几乎全部海外华人的网站，从此无法被国内访问。信仰从此崩塌，我的人生不可挽回的发生了翻天覆地的改变，沉沦，消极，不想上学，甚至怀疑人生。 从此没有碰过任何代码。心情万分沮丧。

今年，一个跟他们星际类似的，没有任何版权宣称的软件，18元一个月的价格吸引我，为了看某部连续剧，我看起了生肉。因为几年前就见过这个原理的软件，我一下子知道这个软件是ss 。这个软件重新挑起了我对代码的种种回忆，我很感谢这个软件，他没有任何ss的版权声明。但我觉得又有什么关系，公司的生存需要资金的支持，不管他们做的如何不对，但是他们的实际行为帮助了我。我不会公开这个公司的名称，就像我不希望星际那帮孩子被这样责问一个心态。 因为我感同身受， 我希望这些孩子做些有意义的事情。给他们些空间吧，让所有归国的热爱网络的人，得到些安慰吧。钱是赚不完的，让他们赚点儿去吧，希望星际那几个人，遵守你们的承诺，为社区做出贡献。 

但是真没必要风风火火的把ss的版权出现在每一个用到这个技术的软件上。我看到的，不是给clowwindy扬名立传，我看到的是如何真正的保护ss 不被圈子外的人知道。 经过包装的软件，正是对ss最好的保护！

有感而发，没有开源精神，仅仅出于这个软件的需求！开源社区，给个绿色通道，放过这些孩子们吧！

from troposphere import Base64, FindInMap, GetAtt
from troposphere import Parameter, Output, Ref, Template
import troposphere.ec2 as ec2


template = Template()

keyname_param = template.add_parameter(Parameter(
    "KeyName",
    Description="Name of an existing EC2 KeyPair to enable SSH "
                "access to the instance",
    Type="String",
))

template.add_mapping('RegionMap', {
    "us-east-1": {"AMI": "ami-7f418316"},
    "us-west-1": {"AMI": "ami-951945d0"},
    "us-west-2": {"AMI": "ami-16fd7026"},
    "eu-west-1": {"AMI": "ami-24506250"},
    "sa-east-1": {"AMI": "ami-3e3be423"},
    "ap-southeast-1": {"AMI": "ami-74dda626"},
    "ap-northeast-1": {"AMI": "ami-dcfa4edd"}
})

ec2_placementgroup = template.add_resource(ec2.PlacementGroup(
    "PlacementGroup",
	Strategy="cluster"
))

ec2_instance1 = template.add_resource(ec2.Instance(
    "Ec2Instance1",
    ImageId=FindInMap("RegionMap", Ref("AWS::Region"), "AMI"),
    InstanceType="t1.micro",
	PlacementGroupName="PlacementGroup",
    KeyName=Ref(keyname_param),
    SecurityGroups=["default"],
    UserData=Base64("80")
))

ec2_instance2 = template.add_resource(ec2.Instance(
    "Ec2Instance2",
    ImageId=FindInMap("RegionMap", Ref("AWS::Region"), "AMI"),
    InstanceType="t1.micro",
	PlacementGroupName="PlacementGroup",
    KeyName=Ref(keyname_param),
    SecurityGroups=["default"],
    UserData=Base64("80")
))

print(template.to_json())
`

In the very, very early days of WAL-E, it backed up and restored 8.4 databases, no doubt. I hypothesize that back then, the `wal_level` setting didn't exist: either you were doing log-shipping (which is what WAL-E is: a log shipper), or you weren't, and if you were log shipping the WAL format grew whatever overhead it needed to to be useful

If in doubt, try a restore, and/or carefully read the sections involving wal-shipping in the 8.4 manual.

The name that Word uses for some fonts is unpredictable. In general, if it doesn't work the way it's spelled in the Word UI, the most direct approach is to use Word to apply the font to some text in a very small document (like a single word in a single paragraph), save the file, and then use opc-diag to examine the document.xml part.

```bash
$ opc browse test-doc.docx document.xml
```

 The proper name to use will appear there, something like this:

```xml
      <w:r w:rsidRPr="00392E92">
        <w:rPr>
          <w:rFonts w:ascii="ヒラギノ角ゴ ProN W3" w:eastAsia="ヒラギノ角ゴ ProN W3" w:hAnsi="ヒラギノ角ゴ ProN W3"/>
        </w:rPr>
        <w:t>Foobar.</w:t>
      </w:r>

```

If they differ, you want the one in `w:ascii` and `w:hAnsi`. I believe python-docx sets them both to the same value.

The reason is, when I cancel the channel import, the annotation of the channel content has a long delay, the time will depend on the content that channel downloaded. @rtibbles also said that it fix should be on the frontend.

Any movement on this? I'd like to have our applications directly consuming secrets and I can't see Confidant being a good choice for that without API docs.

HA is built-into the system assuming you're utilizing AWS. The confidant web service is stateless, using dynamodb for its state and KMS for the encryption/decryption. As long as you run more than one confidant web server and you run them behind an ELB it's HA. It's possible to lose entire AZs without any form of downtime.

Multi-region is a bit more involved to discuss, and depends on how you use confidant.

1. If you want your secrets to be specific to each region, then it's a matter of running confidant in each region and updating the secrets in each. I'd imagine this isn't the type of multi-region most people want/expect, which is some form of replication.
2. If you're using only blind credentials, then the multi-region story is pretty similar to biscuit, because the encryption happens on the client side. The caveat here is that you need to replicate the dynamodb data from one region to the other, which you can do with dynamo streams.
3. If you're using normal credentials, then you need to replicate the dynamo stream, decrypt secrets using the key from one region and rencrypt them using the key from the replicated region. This is something we should provide tooling for.
4. If confidant itself is allowed to cross-region, you can have the confidant web services run in one region while accessing dynamo and kms from the other region.

The downside to both blind credentials and biscuit's approach to multi-region is that if you need a secret to be usable in more than one region you need to encrypt the secret with all the region's KMS keys up-front. If you want to add another region into the mix later, you need to decrypt/reencrypt each secret to be made available in each region.

For "Issue 1" - `A ProgrammingError: Attempt to use a closed cursor`, I believe this is just a behavior difference between `pyodbc` and other database backends.  If you look at [the Django trunk](https://github.com/django/django/blob/f269c1d6f6dcc22c0a781f3223c6da0a4483b06e/django/db/models/sql/compiler.py#L849) for file `django/db/models/sql/compiler.py`, it has these two snippets:   

    849 result = cursor_iter(
    850     cursor, self.connection.features.empty_fetchmany_value,
    851     self.col_count
    852 )
    853 if not self.connection.features.can_use_chunked_reads:
    854     try:
    855         # If we are using non-chunked reads, we return the same data
    856         # structure as normally, but ensure it is all read into memory
    857         # before going any further.
    858         return list(result)
    859     finally:
    860         # done with the cursor
    861         cursor.close()

and the [`cursor_iter()` definition](https://github.com/django/django/blob/f269c1d6f6dcc22c0a781f3223c6da0a4483b06e/django/db/models/sql/compiler.py#L1129) :

    1129 def cursor_iter(cursor, sentinel, col_count):
            [...]
    1134     try:
    1135         for rows in iter((lambda: cursor.fetchmany(GET_ITERATOR_CHUNK_SIZE)),
    1136                          sentinel):
    1137             yield [r[0:col_count] for r in rows]
    1138     finally:
    1139         cursor.close()

849 creates an iterator with a built-in `close()` function.  858 invokes the iterator to completion, calling that `close()` function, and 861 invokes the `close()` function a 2nd time.

So I think the solution might be to wrapper the `cursor`'s `close()` function to silence the `ProgrammingError` exceptions resulting from closing a pyodbc cursor more than once:

    diff --git a/django_pyodbc/base.py b/django_pyodbc/base.py
    index 747f36e..47099ae 100644
    --- a/django_pyodbc/base.py
    +++ b/django_pyodbc/base.py
    @@ -367,6 +367,12 @@ class CursorWrapper(object):
             self.last_params = ()
             self.encoding = encoding
     
    +    def close(self):
    +        try:
    +            self.cursor.close()
    +        except Database.ProgrammingError:
    +            pass
    +
         def format_sql(self, sql, n_params=None):
             if not self.driver_supports_utf8 and isinstance(sql, text_type):
                 # Older FreeTDS (and other ODBC drivers?) don't support Unicode yet, so

Hi @jamesleesaunders, thanks for running the script! It looks like sometimes controller doesn't answer in time, so running it again would exhibit the behavior.

But I think I know what was wrong. I had two bugs, which meant that changing just one thing didn't improve anything:

  - first byte is a sequence number (whatever you set it to, it will be the first byte of response)
  - `AO` was set to 3 instead of 1 (ZDO specs mention that AO should be 1)

With this fixed, I now get:

    REQUEST (tx_explicit): {'profile': '\x00\x00', 'dest_addr_long': '\x00\x13\xA2\x00\x40\xA3\xB7\x2F', 'dest_endpoint': '\x00', 'src_endpoint': '\x00', 'cluster': '\x00\x05', 'dest_addr': '\x00\x00', 'data': '\xAB\x00\x00'}
    RECEIVED: {'profile': '\x00\x00', 'source_addr': '\x00\x00', 'dest_endpoint': '\x00', 'rf_data': **'\xAB\x00\x00\x00\x02\xE6\xE8'**, 'source_endpoint': '\x00', 'options': '\x01', 'source_addr_long': '\x00\x13\xA2\x00\x40\xA3\xB7\x2F', 'cluster': '\x80\x05', 'id': 'rx_explicit'}

This now makes sense:

  - `\xAB` is the sequence number I supplied in request
  - `\x00` is status (presumably "ok", though I couldn't find a reference except for [this](https://github.com/FantomJAC/bekko/blob/0a2ea335fd04870ce433968946c818d7bce5c2e8/bekko-core/src/main/java/com/valleycampus/zigbee/zdo/ZigBeeDevice.java#L34))
  - `\x00\x00` is controller address (always 0x0000)
  - `\x02` is number of endpoints
  - `\xE6\xE8` are two endpoints

Actually, if I had followed XBee User Guide more closely I would have noticed that the ZDO frames have the sequence number at the start (note that ZCL header is different - 3 to 5 bytes, as outlined in ZCL specs). But in my defense, code from desert-home has the same bug... ;)

The "invalid" `rf_data` in responses now makes sense. First byte is whatever I supplied and next one is status (80 is "invalid request", 81 is "device not found"). What follows is the rest of my response and some additional data, depending on status.

So, I think this is solved! I want to thank you all for your responses and for your kind help!

I wrote [this](https://gist.github.com/ajkavanagh/ae7b7dc60f0e6e1595e934a48a023548) awhile back, about what sort of things would be good.  I'll also copy this to #404 and #405.  The initial paragraph reads:

# Dependencies in Reactive charms
## Introduction

In the OpenStack team, and potentially elsewhere, there is a need to maintain stable as well as devel charms. With non-reactive charms, this is fairly simple.

However, with reactive charms, when a charm is rebuilt the dependencies are taken from the master branch of the various layers and interfaces, and the charm-helpers library, which is baked into layer-basic, is the latest available on PyPi.

The issue is serious: the OpenStack is unable to support stable/17.02 charms without completely manually rebuilding them based on downloading all of the layers and manually editing wheelhouse.txt files to ensure that the versions that were available at the time the stable was cut are the correct versions, along with any cherry picked patches.

The builds are starting to fail less than six months after the original freeze to stable, which means that patches cannot be cherry-picked back to stable charms.

```sh
Available kernels:

  clojure    /opt/conda/share/jupyter/kernels/clojure
  groovy     /opt/conda/share/jupyter/kernels/groovy

And by inspecting the `kernel.json` file at the directory specified, you can see the bootstrapped version of python a kernel is invoking:

```sh
$ cat /usr/local/share/jupyter/kernels/sas/kernel.json

   "argv" : [
      "/opt/conda/bin/python",
      "-m",
      "sas_kernel",
      "-f",
      "{connection_file}"
   ],
   "codemirror_mode" : "sas",
   "display_name" : "SAS",
   "language" : "sas"
}

$ cat /opt/conda/share/jupyter/kernels/python3/kernel.json 
{
 "argv": [
  "/opt/conda/bin/python",
  "-m",
  "ipykernel",
  "-f",
  "{connection_file}"
 ],
 "display_name": "Python 3",
 "language": "python"
}
```

So my sas and python3 kernels both use the conda installation (`/opt/conda/bin/python`) -- but I bring this up because it is entirely possible to have, eg., your `python3` kernel invoking a different system installation of python than your `sas` kernel, in which case the modules (version of saspy etc) could also be different.

Regarding 3. A more precise `timedelta` won't make a difference to us. We will rely on duck typing so we won't need to make any changes.

One reason for keeping it is discoverability. By using it in our examples we show that the timeout, delay, holdoff type parameters are different that in C. If you're used to the nidmm `fetch()` taking an integer number of milliseconds, then if the only obvious difference is in Python it takes a float, you have to know the somehow that the units is in seconds. If it takes a float or `timedelta`, then it becomes more obvious that something is different.

Yeah, just googling "bach invention sheet music with fingerings" will bring up many results. Here's [one](http://www.freesheetmusic.net/bach/15%20Inventions.pdf). Standard scores do not contain fingerings for every single note. But they usually contain the ones where there could be a question, and the rest are assumed to be the finger closest at hand. 
I would push back on the idea that fingerings are largely subjective. While two high level players might disagree on a few notes within a piece, they would agree on almost all of them, and the rest they would recognize as "another good option". Any good algorithm should be able to be in that ballpark as well. Where even if a high level player disagrees, they would say, "sure I might do it like this, but the algorithm picked another reasonable option."

I think allowing the hand to dynamically adapt it's rest-position will be a great improvement. 

Separately, when I was writing my algorithm, I found it very useful to write tests for certain cases. It revealed various flaws, and helped me improve the algorithm.

Thanks! arXiv version is [here](https://arxiv.org/abs/1609.00158).

This would not exactly be trivial to add to trackpy, but still may be worthwhile. It looks like the [MATLAB implementation] (https://arxiv.org/src/1609.00158v1/anc/Tracker.m) is not a true global minimizer, but instead uses the equivalent of `search_range` to make a first cut. So matching this implementation in trackpy would involve writing a new subnet solver function (aka `link_strategy`). The only architectural difference would be that this subnet solver would need access to all particle positions, in order to consider particles' nearest neighbors and compute the cost function. That's not so bad. The actual minimization (the hard part) could be done by the existing "core" subnet-solving algorithm, or by calling an existing Hungarian algorithm implementation as the authors do.

This algorithm looks like it might be a little slow compared to trackpy's prediction feature. However, it could supplant prediction for many users because it requires so little configuration.

This issue was moved to deis/builder#467

>  I don't recall seeing this error in earlier versions, but to be fair I didn't do a lot of testing of the endpoints while building.

This has always been Deis' behaviour when it comes to deploying a new version. By default, the old containers are killed off as soon as fleet says the new version is up, which essentially means the container has booted. However, if your new container takes a while to actually boot (database migrations, initialization etc.), 504s will occur. That's where HEALTHCHECK_URL comes in. We didn't want to enforce this policy by default because it looks for a 200 OK at the root (/), which some applications might not respond as expected and that would be a breaking change. Therefore, you need to opt into this. You might also want to take a look into setting HEALTHCHECK_INITIAL_DELAY as well if you know how long it takes between the container booting and your app listening for incoming requests, as by default it's set to zero so health checks occur immediately.

> In general, should all apps have healthchecks now?

It depends on if you care about zero-downtime deploys. If so then yes, all your apps should have health checks. If that's not much of a concern and you care more about a faster deployment pipeline, then you should leave it unset.

Hope this helps!

We aren't exactly "serializing" the dict here, it's just preparing it for json encoding. I can't imagine this being used for anything other than calling something that converts the dict to a json object next.

[Another option](https://stackoverflow.com/a/26961568/8412986) is to make an alternative to `jsonify` that accepts a json str:

```py
def json_response(json_str):
    return app.response_class(
        response=json_str,
        status=200,
        mimetype='application/json'
    )
    
def my_endpoint():
    combined_str = Web3.toJSON(combined_data)
    return json_response(combined_str)
```

This seems more generally usable across frameworks, namable, and easy to document as a public Web3 API. Otherwise, it is "Web3.convert_to_a_dict_that_is_json_encodable(obj)"...

Worst case, people can `json.loads()` from the str, if they *need* a dict that is json encodable, but not json encoded (but I think those cases are few).

---

If you feel like doing a `toJSON()` implementation, I'm for it. Or if you would rather do the hex mapping, then let's just close this PR.

Nowadays it seems that people just open a shell, activate an env and open the IDE. I think this is far from ideal, because it makes difficult to deal with many env's (you would have to restart the IDE to switch).

Also, depending on what the activate.d scripts do you can have problems to execute the IDE on an activated environment. Let's say, for example, that one of these scripts change the PATH, so it changes the IDE behaviour. Or you have a java package, which breaks java based IDE's.
Unless the IDE or any other tool you want to use is also a conda package there is not guarantee that it will work on an activated env.

Well, all my custom files go into `~/.config/fish`, but fish also places some stuff in `~/.local/share/fish`.

According to this [issue](https://github.com/fish-shell/fish-shell/issues/1956) in their GH, there are more locations involved, as commented in this [commit](https://github.com/fish-shell/fish-shell/commit/c1b384e5d3f2f46e7d7a1b41e31c7aadc1d9ce28):

> Right after reading /usr/share/fish/config.fish and before reading /etc/fish/config.fish, fish will also read files in ~/.config/fish/conf.d/, /etc/fish/conf.d and /usr/share/fish/vendor_conf.d (the exact values depend on $XDG_CONFIG_HOME, $__fish_sysconfdir and $__fish_datadir). If there are files with the same name in two or all of these, fish will only attempt to read the first (skipping all files with that name if it is unreadable). ~/.config takes precedence over /etc/ which takes precedence over /usr. The path to the latter can also be gotten via `pkg-config` as "confdir", and is meant for third-party applications to integrate with fish.

Hope it helps!

The example I gave above was probably bad.  Pure-python files inside of a `site-packages`-like directory (or using `PYTHONPATH` to point at one) will work fine in that case.  In general though, many binaries and executables will probably be broken inside the container, for the same reason you can't just use `mv` to change the location of a conda environment (you have you use `conda create --clone` instead).

The better example is keeping the path the same inside and outside the container.  So

1. create environment outside container with `conda create -p /home/erik/my-work-env`
2. start container with volume `-v /home/erik:/home/erik`
3. install packages with `conda install -p /home/erik/my-work-env numpy`

Yeah I know where the bug is.  4.2.11 introduced the requirement that every channel have a "name."  At least one directory level beyond the host and port.  I guess I didn't remember #3235, and was hoping everyone had at least one directory layer.

We need this to allow channels to have locations.  That is, we need to uniquely identify channels, but they also need to be able to move locations.  Each channel needs a deterministic name.  4.2.11 does that, but it assumes at least one directory level beyond the network location.

The first and most obvious problem is that the Sphinx docs don't include the `VariantRecordSample` object, so I'll definitely look into that.

In the mean time: The `VariantRecordSample` object implements a mapping-like object for a specific VCF/BCF sample row and column.  The keys are FORMAT fields and the values are the values in the VCF/BCF file.  There is special handling for "GT", through the `allele`, `allele_indices` and `phased` attributes.  There is also a `name` property that provides the sample's name.  Obviously, there are dozens of useful attributes and properties that could be added that would be of general utility, some part of htslib and others that are logical extensions.  I just haven't gotten to doing that yet.

A minimal version of our code is below.  I left out things like validating `args`, creating the `targetIndex`, writing the mappings, and  modifying index settings (`refresh_interval`, etc...).  The transform method just removes an unwanted field before passing along to `bulk()`.  Note that we are seeing this behavior even when not passing in a `transform` argument.  We're using the following optional args:

* `args.eshost`: `127.0.0.1`
* `args.clusterTimeout`: `180`
* `args.chunksize`: `500`
* `args.scroll`: `5s`

I'm fairly certain this is an issue with `scroll`/`scan`, as we're seeing the exact same issue as https://discuss.elastic.co/t/scrolling-performance/14560 .  That thread doesn't describe the fix they used to solve this.  I've put in some debug statements and have verified that `scan` is being properly set, which according to everything I read should be what avoids this issue.  You can see that `scan` is being set as the `search_type` in the initial query:

`{u'search_type': 'scan', u'fields': '_source,_parent,_routing,_timestamp', u'scroll': '5s'} `

Code sample below:
```
from elasticsearch import Elasticsearch, exceptions
import threading
from time import sleep, time

def reIndex(args):
    def drawProgressBar(actual, expected, barLen=20):
        percent = 1 if expected == 0 else float(actual) / float(expected)
        progress = ""
        for i in range(barLen):
            if i < int(barLen * percent):
                progress += "="
            else:
                progress += " "
        fmt = "      Docs Indexed: %d / %d  [ %s ] %.2f%%    \r"
        sys.stdout.write(fmt % (actual, expected, progress, percent * 100))
        sys.stdout.flush()

    sourceCluster = Elasticsearch(
        hosts=args.eshost, 
        timeout=args.clusterTimeout, 
        max_retries=4, 
        retry_on_timeout=True)

    targetCluster = Elasticsearch(
        hosts=args.targetCluster, 
        timeout=args.clusterTimeout, 
        max_retries=4,
        retry_on_timeout=True)

    # if source and target cluster are the same, we don't need to specify the target
    # to the reindexer
    workerTargetClient = None if sourceCluster == targetCluster else targetCluster

    # count docs in source index so we can make sure we've copied them all
    sourceCount = sourceCluster.count(args.sourceIndex)['count']

    class ReIndexTransforms(object):
        @classmethod
        def transformES2(cls, action):
            docType = action['_type']
            docSource = action['_source']

            # remove erroneous 'oldField' field from some types
            if docType == 'account' or docType == 'user' and 'oldField' in docSource:
                docSource.pop('oldField', None)

            return cls.packageTransform(action, docSource)

        @classmethod
        def packageTransform(cls, action, source):
            actionLine = {
                'index': {
                    '_index': action['_index'],
                    '_type': action['_type'],
                    '_id': action['_id']
                }
            }

            # optional fields
            if '_parent' in action:
                actionLine['index']['_parent'] = action['_parent']
            if '_routing' in action:
                actionLine['index']['_routing'] = action['_routing']

            return (actionLine, source)

    ReIndexTransforms.LOOKUP = {
        'transformES2': {
            'doc': ReIndexTransforms.transformES2,
            'mappings': ReIndexTransforms.transformES2Mappings,
        },
    }

    def _doReindexWork():
        callback = ReIndexTransforms.LOOKUP.get(args.transform, {}).get('doc')
        bulkArgs = dict(expand_action_callback=callback) if callback else {}
        es_helpers.reindex(
            sourceCluster,
            args.sourceIndex,
            args.targetIndex,
            target_client=workerTargetClient,
            chunk_size=args.chunkSize,
            scroll=args.scroll,
            bulk_kwargs=bulkArgs)

    # start the hard work!
    worker = threading.Thread(target=_doReindexWork)
    worker.daemon = True  # so we can ctrl-c out if necessary
    worker.start()
    while worker.is_alive():
        drawProgressBar(targetCluster.count(args.targetIndex)['count'], sourceCount)
        sleep(1)

    # do an explicit refresh to make sure count api is accurate
    targetCluster.indices.refresh(args.targetIndex)

    # draw final progress bar to 100%
    drawProgressBar(targetCluster.count(args.targetIndex)['count'], sourceCount)

    print "\nReindexing complete!"
```

public ip prefix is a means to reserve a CIDR prefix of a specific length. the reservation of this range guarantees a sequential set of addresses.  This in turn makes a number of things easier.  Rather than having a random set of public IP's, customers can describe the destination or source of their traffic with a single prefix.  So, if your deployment has a need for 64 IP's, you can whitelist the entire range of 64 IP's with one statement. If you didn't have the prefix as the source for public IP's, and instead have 64 individual addresses, they are very unlikely to be aggregate-able/summarized as a single prefix because they are random and not in a contiguous range.    In turn, the tradeoff is a single statement or 64 statements; the latter has scaling issues for customers.  It's a very clean way to create a surface area of contiguous IP address space.

customers can then take the prefix resource and use it in two ways:

1) create individual public IP resource from it by referencing the prefix resource.  then use those public ip's like any other public ip's
2) assign the prefix directly to an outbound rule, and the outbound rule will use all ip's in the reserved range.

If (1) was started, (2) is not allowed.  It's an either or choice.

From an outbound rules perspective, one or more public ip resources, one or more prefix, or a combination of both can be assigned to outbound rules.

New script that will iterate until a CancelledError is raised. Useful to check which versions are affected:

```python
import sys
import logging
import asyncio
import aiohttp
import concurrent


logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


class Service:
    def __init__(self):
        self.session = aiohttp.ClientSession()

    async def do(self, timeout):
        async with self.session.get('https://api.github.com/events', timeout=timeout) as resp:
            logger.warning("%s, %s", timeout, resp.status)


async def view():
    service1 = Service()
    service2 = Service()
    try:
        await service1.do(0.0000001)
    except asyncio.TimeoutError:
        logger.exception("Caught TimeoutError")

    try:
        await service2.do(20)
    except concurrent.futures.CancelledError:
        logger.exception("GOT CANCELLED")
        sys.exit(0)


async def iterate_view():
    for x in range(100):
        await view()

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.run_until_complete(iterate_view())
```

Well, it's simple. 
1) Becase we can (: It's a good hint for your parser to know how much data to read before part is over. Semantic of `Content-Length` is pretty known and standard.

2) While none of RFC allows to do that, none one forbids to do that. Unexpectable headerss should be ignored per RFC.

3) Originally, this behavior was borrowed from CouchDB which [sends](http://docs.couchdb.org/en/2.1.1/api/document/common.html#efficient-multiple-attachments-retrieving) multipart body parts with attachments with Content-Length header set. From CouchDB side this is a way to preserve attachment meta information which includes blob`s number of bytes. So your client/server can do the same.

Probably, we can drop it and feel no sorry, but imagine the case you receive multipart payload with some text and images and you need to decide to do preview or not depending on their size. Much likely you wouldn't let do preview for 3+ MiB images, while small one you would like to. Content-Length header could help you to make a decision long before you start read body part data. You can even skip it with easy - you know how long you should leap to start read the next one.

So, it's very helpful hint for your clients and servers. You can ignore it if you like, but when you'll need it - it'll be there. I think 14+X{1-6} additional bytes per body part is not a big price for such opportunities.

Thank you for the summary @mpacer. As is pointed out, I'm both against frontends using tags as part of their state AND an offender as a papermill contributor / user. To bring clarity to my hypocrisy, I'll explain:

* The UI/UX for setting _new_ kinds of metadata tends to be hard for users (since the built-in option is the JSON editor if new UI isn't created for it).
* I _really_ wanted papermill to use top level metadata for parameters. However, the notebook wouldn't necessarily be executable then. We need to be able to have default parameters recognized by frontends that don't know our format. The easiest way to do that is with a plain code cell. We could easily switch to a boolean flag for metadata on the cell once we've started customizing a bit more.
* As a frontend implementer, the metadata object can be well typed and specced. Arrays of arbitrary strings of arbitrary order can't.

It's definitely not a good example for password storage, but a collision attack is not a plausible vector for stealing a password - an attacker would have to trick you into picking a specific long and unlikely password, which they would have to know, in order to generate a different password that results in the same hash. And if someone can persuade you to use a password they know, the collision is irrelevant. It's a good idea to move to a stronger hash anyway, but the recent news doesn't make that particularly urgent.

A collision attack (making two inputs which produce the same hash) is generally much easier to do than a preimage attack (making an input which produces a given hash). The former is what has happened for SHA-1, and I don't believe it makes much difference for this use case.

The test execution order appears to be relevant here. Duplicating `test_derive_key_oversized_cryptographic_length` and renaming it so it appears earlier in the execution order causes the error to disappear. Renaming it so that it appears later in the execution order doesn't work however; the error still appears. Renaming (not duplicating) `test_derive_key_oversized_cryptographic_length` so that it appears earlier in the execution order transfers the error; it now appears under `test_derive_key_alternate_derivation_data`.

Additional experimentation with including/excluding individual test methods also yields interesting results. Removing (i.e., commenting out) any one of the new `test_decrypt...` methods causes the error to disappear. In fact, removing any of the other tests in the `TestKmipEngine` suite causes the error to disappear, except for the one skipped test, `test_derive_key_unspecified_iv` (which is an empty test).

These experiments indicate that there is some underlying issue with how the `sqlalchemy` sessions and their associated object instances are being handled across the entire test suite, independent of which actual test is at fault during any given test run.

For the "'add-attribute' and 'get-attribute-list'" patch (44aef33), like #167 this should be broken up into multiple smaller patches for simplifying testing and review. This isn't explicitly codified in any of the project docs but my guideline for how to scope a patch (or pull request) is simply: add a single feature and the test cases required to verify that it works and fails as expected. The goal is 100% code coverage for newly submitted code, though this is not a super strict requirement.

For 44aef33, the code should probably be broken up like this:
* a pull request adding the `AddAttribute` payloads and tests
* a pull request adding support for `AddAttribute` payloads to the payload factories and tests
* a pull request adding `Query` demo utilities (no tests needed)
* a pull request adding `AddAttribute` demos and demo utilities (no tests needed)
* a pull request adding `AddAttributeResult` and tests (these probably don't exist for other result objects)
* a pull request adding `AddAttribute` to the KMIPProxy client and tests
* a pull request adding `AddAttribute` to the `ProxyKmipClient` and tests
* a pull request adding the `get_attribute_list` utility method to the Pie object hierarchy and tests
* a pull request adding `GetAttributeList` to the `KmipEngine` and tests
* a pull request adding `AddAttribute` to the `KmipEngine` and tests
* a pull request adding placeholder support for `GetAttributes` to the `KmipEngine` and tests
* a pull request adding placeholder support for `ModifyAttribute` to the `KmipEngine` and tests

It's definitely easier to write multiple features in a batch. Long term, splitting the submission up like this makes it easier to review the project history and to work with individual features without changing everything else around them.

For "kmip:demos: import pkcs12" patch (1cc0500), it's not clear to me yet if this is still a work-in-progress or if it's ready for review. Regardless, the commit itself should be split out into a separate pull request that references this one.

To answer your first question, you can use the configuration file or manually provide the settings with the `KMIPProxy` constructor. Note that if you use both, the settings provided with the constructor take priority.

Regarding the certificate verification failure, I've seen a couple issues in the past that could be related. The first is a permissions issue, where the permissions on the certificate/key/CA files themselves are blocking your Python process from reading and using the certificate data. Double check that you can open and read each of those files from Python to make sure this isn't the problem.

The second is a settings issue, where the settings on your KMIP server are not configured to match the certificate you are trying to use. Contact your server admin and try to determine if the CA file used by the server is valid for the client certificate you are using. If it is not, the server will not be able to verify your certificate and the TLS connection will fail.

In my experience, TLS certificate debugging is consistently the most troublesome aspect of setting up PyKMIP for new users, since so many subtle things can go wrong. I've thought about setting up a TLS-specific FAQ to help users through this process. Would you find that useful?

Looks good. Next up is FileStore. Steps will be:

1. Use the databroker method `get_resource_uids` to identify which resource documents we need to copy.
2. Get these resource documents from `self.fs` and insert them into `fs`.
3. Use a (yet to be written) method on filestore to identify which datum documents we need to copy, and insert them into `fs` as well.

At this point, the `mds` and `fs` will work correctly, but `fs` still refers to files (like TIFFs) that are on the local filesystem. We need to copy the underlying files to a new location and then update `fs` to point there instead.

4. Add an additional required parameter to the signature of `db.export` named `new_root`.
5. Copy the files into the `new_root` directory using a (yet to be written) method on filestore handlers.
6. Update the copied resource documents in the new `fs` using `change_root`.

Some unicode characters require two (16-bit) code points to be represented. These characters consist of a pair of characters: a high surrogate and a low surrogate. I strongly suspect that Satellite is correctly decoding these code point pairs as a single character, which is triggering the "whitespace" bug. We can work around it by making sure that we always generate strings at least four code points long, which is what this PR does.

VS for Mac supports the new .NET Core `csproj` format introduced in VS 2017 RC. It does not support the old `xproj` format. As mentioned earlier you can migrate `xproj` to `csproj` on the commandline using `dotnet migrate`.

An update to the VS 2017 RC made further simplification to project files using a new MSBuild concept called SDKs, as explained here:
https://blogs.msdn.microsoft.com/dotnet/2016/12/12/updating-visual-studio-2017-rc-net-core-tooling-improvements

You can identify `csproj` projects that use the SDK feature by the `Sdk` attribute in the toplevel `Project` attribute and a lack of `<Import>` elements. VS for Mac (as of Preview 2) does not yet support MSBuild SDKs, but we're working on adding it.

> Too many certificates already issued for: domain.org

There is a [rate limit](https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/beta-program-announcements/1631) of 5 subdomains in a 7 day period.  So, if you request

* <https://example.com>
* <https://www.example.com>
* <https://blog.example.com>
* <https://api.example.com>
* <https://forum.example.com>
* <https://support.example.com>

then you have made 6 requests for <http://*.example.com> and the last one will fail.  The error message tells you the base domain for which the limit applies - not the sub-domain which you requested.

Try again in 7 days...

It's a real problem if you use subdomains for internationalisation (<https://en.example.com>, <https://fr.example.com>, <https://de.example.com>, ...)  - but it's a free service, and they do say they will reduce the rate limit once things settle down...

Were you looking for the certificates here? By default, they are stored in `/etc/letsencrypt`. More specifically, the symlinks in `/etc/letsencrypt/live/<domain>/` point to the most recently issued certificate/chain/fullchain and the most recently used private key.

If you're certificates aren't due for renewal, this command has no effect. You can change this behavior by providing `--force-renewal` on the command line.

That's a sound plan, @davidism . A few comments:

> validate(self, data, errors) performs schema-level validation...  It puts messages in the errors dict, and returns nothing.

- For backwards-compatibility, we could name this `validate_schema`.
- Instead of mutating the errors dict, I'm thinking the method should raise a `ValidationError`, just like a normal validator. This would, however, be limiting because only one error could be raised. The benefit of the "hooks-as-decorators" API is that multiple schema validators can be attached, each one raising its own error.

> This also means that there's no separate "external" validate vs. load methods, 

I think the `validate` method is still useful when you want to validate input data without doing expensive deserialization work (e.g. database access). 

> validate_<field name>(self, value) 

I've never really liked this API in Django forms and WTForms. It is redundant API and a bit too "magical". I think we can defer implementing this.

> accessor is removed. Getting the data in the right format is the job of pre_dump now.

`accessor` is still necessary because it defines how to *pull a single value from an object* (e.g. pulling a value by key from a `dict`, accessing an attribute/property). It does not do any preprocessing.

> This means there's no way to return data and errors at the same time. 

Can you clarify this? Are you proposing to change the return value of `load` and `dump`?

I'm wondering if there's some incompatibility between R's and Python's HDF5 implementations then. I used Seurat's [Read10X](https://github.com/satijalab/seurat/blob/master/R/preprocessing.R#L174) function, which returns a sparse matrix with cell IDs as colnames and gene names are rownames, and loomR's [create](https://github.com/mojaveazure/loomR/blob/develop/R/loom.R#L1015) function to make the individual loom files.
```R
library(loomR)
library(Seurat)

root.dir <- '/home/paul/tabula_muris/droplet'
loom.dir <- file.path(dirname(path = root.dir), 'looms')
if (!dir.exists(paths = loom.dir)) {
  dir.create(path = loom.dir, showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)
}
data.dir <- list.dirs(path = root.dir)
data.dir <- data.dir[which(x = data.dir != root.dir)]

for (dir in data.dir) {
  cat('\n', "Working on", basename(path = dir), '\n')
  name <- basename(path = dir)
  expr <- Read10X(data.dir = dir)
  lfile <- create(
    filename = file.path(loom.dir, paste0(name, '.loom')),
    data = t(x = expr),
    overwrite = TRUE
  )
  lfile$close_all()
  gc(verbose = FALSE)

Your suggestion of a queue and mapping is quite similar to what's implemented right now; we call them DecRefQueues and they collect the native pointers when a non-native Z3Object like an AST is destroyed. The queue is kept until someone can dec-ref them (concurrently, so the queues have locks). In the current implementation, every Context has a number of DecRefQueues for different types of objects.

If you want to you can try to workaround this by trying Z3's CMake based build system if you want where the ".NET" bindings should build on Windows.

Here are the steps to the build

1. Install [CMake](https://cmake.org/download/) if you don't already have it.
2. Install [Ninja](https://github.com/ninja-build/ninja/releases) if you don't have already have it. Although you can use `nmake` if you really want to (CMake supports it). Ninja is **much much** better because it runs in parallel giving very fast build times.

3. In your Z3 git repository run the following

```
git clean -fxd
```

This removes unwanted files left behind by the other build system.

Now copy the CMake files into the correct place in the git repository

```
python contrib/cmake/bootstrap.py create
```

4. Now make a new build directory. I'm assuming this is created inside the root directory of the git repository but you can create it any where you like on your system

```
mkdir build
```

5. Now run CMake to generate the build system. Note this should be run from a console where the C++ and C# compiler are available. Typically that requires you to run the `vcvarsall.bar x64` command in your console.

```
cd build
cmake -G Ninja -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=RelWithDebInfo -DBUILD_DOTNET_BINDINGS=ON ..\
```

Note if you didn't put your `build` directory in the git repository replace `..\` with the full path to the Z3 git repository on your system. If your're curious about the available configuration options read the `README-CMake.md` file in the Z3 repository.

6. Finally start the build. Just run

```
ninja
```

in your build directory.

Hope that helps.

So you have about 14M java.lang.ref.PhantomReference objects around. 
As far as I understand these are references to Z3 expressions (6M+ of which are Boolean expressions).
"Someone" would have to tell the GC to collect these as the context object would not go away unless the expressions that are owned by the context go away.
To my understanding there are two possible approaches: (1) Batfish keeps track of the expressions it has created and ensures the dispose/finalize methods are called by the scope exit (2) the Java runtime uses a double indirection to the context object instead of the single indirection. When the context is disposed, the set of allocated reference counts to ast objects are decremented at the time the context is finalized. Dangling expression objects are no longer valid as the underlying context pointer is now set to null. @cheshire what is your take?

**From the hardware perspective:** There are two related instructions `FPREM` and `FPREM1` in the x86 instruction set. Former truncates towards zero (justifying the result `+3.0`), latter rounds to nearest (justifying the result `-1`).

For details, see here: http://www.intel.com/Assets/en_US/PDF/manual/253666.pdf
Volume 2A; pages: 3-398 thru 3-403.

**From the software perspective:** The C function `fmod` promises the result to have the same sign as the first argument, which suggests a `truncate-towards-zero` interpretation. See here: http://linux.die.net/man/3/fmod

I'll posit that the SMT-Lib standard models (i.e., `FPREM`/`fmod`) and hence `+3.0` is the correct answer in this case. Perhaps the most obvious thing to do would be to change the standard so that `fp.rem` actually takes the rounding-mode as an argument, allowing modeling both `FPREM` and `FPREM1` and whatever other rounding modes one might want.

Yes absolutely since any graph of cells can be accepted. I've implemented it below which runs, can't attest to it's correctness but the resulting shape is right. (It's slightly hackish since `CustomRecurrentCell` is used in a way it's not designed to, but that can be fixed.)

    num_batch, seq_len, n_features = 2, 3, 4
    x_in = np.random.random((num_batch, seq_len, n_features)).astype('float32')
    l_inp = InputLayer((num_batch, seq_len, n_features))

    lasagne.random.get_rng().seed(1234)
    cell_inp = InputLayer((num_batch, n_features))
    cell_inp_dummy = InputLayer((num_batch, n_features))
    cell = CustomRecurrentCell(
        cell_inp_dummy,
        ExpressionLayer(cell_inp_dummy, lambda x: T.zeros((num_batch, n_features))),
        DenseLayer(cell_inp_dummy, n_features))['output']
    cell = ConcatLayer([cell, cell_inp])
    l_rec = lasagne.layers.RecurrentContainerLayer(
        {cell_inp: l_inp}, cell, precompute_input=False)

    output = helper.get_output(l_rec).eval({l_inp.input_var: x_in})

The director doesn't guarantee any sort of path following -- it just tries to push execution toward a given goal. Likewise, the Oppologist *really* doesn't reason about where it should go -- it simply catches errors from our VEX SimEngine and runs our unicorn SimEngine instead. Neither of these techniques will stick to a slice...

Regarding your second point, exhausting all possible paths through the program is not something that current techniques can do. As an example, consider the following program: `while (getchar()) { }`. This program has infinitely many paths, unless you do some constraining to limit them.

We don't have built-in timeout support, but you can probably use something like https://pypi.python.org/pypi/timeout-decorator wrap your exploration in a timeout.

I want to generate a proxy as per the DIRAC config and submit a job. The aptly named ```check_proxy``` command in LCG calls for the proxy to be renewed regardless of how valid it is.

Steps to reproduce:
Change the gridproxy commands in the config to be anything and the GLITE system will not be able to run with this as the GLITE system requests for a proxy to be generated on the import of the class.


If GLITE was being run after the schema had finalized then it would work or if the Schema was able to change the default value of the Credentials but it can't before the LCG is imported.
A quick-fix may be to guarantee the import of Credentials is done early enough that it's defaults in the Schema are changed before the LCG system is imported but this requires understanding the order of all of the imports and cross-dependencies and that the Schema doing what we want.

The problem here may be that the schema is defined by:

```python
_schema = Schema(Version(1, 2), {'id': SimpleItem(),
                                 'status': SimpleItem()})
```
and so at creation-time, the attributes are put into a Python `dict`. Even if this is then passed off to an OrderedDict, it is too late to maintain the same order. The class' schema would have to be changed to be defined as:

```python
_schema = Schema(Version(1, 2), OrderedDict([('id', SimpleItem()),
                                             ('status', SimpleItem())]))
```
which is perhaps starting to get a bit invasive.

Once we are on Python 3, we will be able to change `GangaObject`'s metaclass to store attributes in an `OrderedDict` and declare the items directly in the class body.

OK I've taken a look. Here are a few pieces of feedback:

1. For future bug reports (not just for Pact), please provide instructions (e.g. a README) on how to run the reproducible code, it wasn't immediately obvious to me (a non-native python programmer), although I'm handy enough
1. You should set the path in your test to `/minimal-rx/v1/admin/healthcheck` instead of `/`
1. You should update the verification path base url, otherwise you won't be able to properly test other API endpoints on different paths
1. As a general rule, you should test your provider against a local instance of a provider e.g. `src/provider.py` instead of a live endpoint on a real test environment. Otherwise you'll run into other issues with this sort of testing down the line (e.g. managing provider states, or stubbing downstream dependecies)

The problem, from what I can gather, is that you are pointing to a domain with a self-signed certificate (https://alignapi.alta-sqa1.aligntech.com/) in your provider verification, and the process is failing because of it. I could make your tests work locally by extracting the expected response and putting into your test provider, and running the verification against it.

See the "pact-provider-verifier" section at https://github.com/pact-foundation/pact-ruby-standalone/releases on how to specify a custom SSL for the verifier.

You will probably need to talk to a Developer/Ops person in your org to assist you in finding this certificate and setting up your environment.

That being said, my suggestion is that you don't hit the live environment and hit a local one instead, thus side stepping the SSL issue.

I wrote an interface that exposes the model primary key:

```
import graphene


class DjangoPkInterface(graphene.Interface):
    """
    Exposes the Django model primary key
    """
    pk = graphene.ID(description="Primary key")

    def resolve_pk(self, args, context, info):
        return self.pk
```

You can expose model properties by defining resolvers, as I did above.

@mwilniewiec, thanks for reporting this issue!

I agree that the Thread specification does not address how to handle RLOC changes.  The Thread Group is tracking this issue in [SPEC-490](https://threadgroup.atlassian.net/browse/SPEC-490), but that has gone stale.

I do agree that, at a minimum, that the Leader should update the Border Agent Locator TLV when noticing a change via the Keep Alive messages.  I've submitted #2596 to address this issue.

However, even with #2596, changes in the RLOC will go unnoticed until the next Keep Alive message is sent.  I see two options:
1. Send a Keep Alive message immediately after a role change.
2. Do not allow role changes while the device is a Commissioner.

Thoughts?

I thought about your concern when implementing this.  Unfortunately, the Thread specification is not clear on what to do when the Address Query Set is full.

Defaulting to sending an Address Query when the Address Query Set is full has the benefit that the IPv6 datagram has potential to be delivered to its final destination.  However, it also has the downside that no rate limiting takes place.  The Thread spec only defines a rate limiting mechanism based on a given Address Query entry.  However, the Thread spec does not define a "global" rate limiting mechanism that applies when the Address Query Set is full.

In the end, I chose the conservative method that avoids the possibility of continuously flooding the network with Address Query multicasts.  Happy to hear other thoughts.

Hello @bukepo i was able to reproduce the issue on one of our Linux machine.

**Background**:

It seems that J-Link emulator on-board implements 6 USB endpoints (EPs) that can be used. 
Unfortunately, only 4 of them support USB packet sizes of up to 512 bytes. The EPs used for VCOM are the ones that do not support 512 byte packet sizes but only 64 bytes.

Strangly this seems not to be a problem on Windows, but only on Linux and probably on MAC.

More info here: [https://wiki.segger.com/J-Link-OB_SAM3U](https://wiki.segger.com/J-Link-OB_SAM3U)

**Solution**:

As i mentioned previously it helps if you disable MSD drag & drop feature and then **power cycle the board**.

**Logs**:

With MSD Enabled:

```
b'panid 0xface\r\nDone\r\n> \r\n> '
b'ifconfig up\r\nDone\r\n> \r\n> '
b'thread start\r\nDone\r\n> \r\n> '
b'USBSO\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00 40 activetimestaUSBSO\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00'
```

With MSD Disabled (tested on J-link 5.12j and 6.20i and on dev-kits 0.9.2 and 0.10.0):

```
b'panid 0xface\r\nDone\r\n> \r\n> '
b'ifconfig up\r\nDone\r\n> \r\n> '
b'thread start\r\nDone\r\n> \r\n> '
b'dataset mgmtsetcommand pending pendingtimestamp 40 activetimestamp 80 delaytimer 60000 channel 16 panid 45006\r\nDone\r\n> \r\n> '
```

I modified your Python script a little bit to include larger delay after `thread start` command is called so that node may chance to become Child or Leader.

I guess alternative solution if you would like to use MSD feature is to overwrite **write** command to fragment the input into several 64-bytes long chunks, separated by small delay. Or obviously use UART pins directly without J-Link.

oh, i get it, it is because your function predict_new_velocity() define a graph, so calling it again, will again add ops to the default graph.

You need to encapsulate the graph definition into a new graph, so it avoid any conflict with a predefined graph:
```
def predict_new_velocity(x):
    with tf.Graph().as_default():
        # Building deep neural network
        input_layer = tflearn.input_data(shape=[None, 360, 3])
        dense1 = tflearn.fully_connected(input_layer, 64, activation='relu',
                                        regularizer='L2', weight_decay=0.001)
        dropout1 = tflearn.dropout(dense1, 0.5)
        dense2 = tflearn.fully_connected(dropout1, 64, activation='relu',
                                        regularizer='L2', weight_decay=0.001)
        dropout2 = tflearn.dropout(dense2, 0.5)
        softmax = tflearn.fully_connected(dropout2, 8, activation='softmax')

        # Regression using SGD with learning rate decay and Top-3 accuracy
        sgd = tflearn.SGD(learning_rate=0.1, lr_decay=0.96, decay_step=1000)
        top_k = tflearn.metrics.Top_k(3)
        net = tflearn.regression(softmax, optimizer=sgd, metric=top_k,
                                loss='categorical_crossentropy')

        # load the trained model
        model = tflearn.DNN(net, tensorboard_verbose=3)
        model.load("model.tflearn")

        predict_y = model.predict(x)
        new_y = np.argmax(predict_y, axis=1)
        return new_y.astype(np.uint8)
```

For further optimization, you can simply load graph once, store the DNN class in a global variable for example, and just call it, so you do not need to redefined graph and load weights at every prediction.

`unchecked` is meant to be a bit lower level than that: think of it basically pointer access with stride and shape math (if you're familiar with Eigen, it's basically doing the same thing as an `Eigen::Map`, which similarly doesn't keep its referenced matrix alive).  While it *could* be safe to hold those pointers (not just the data: also the strides and shape) whether or not your unchecked-using code would be safe or not would depend on how your method was called from Python, which seems pretty undesirable.  lvalue-qualifying the method is a cheap way to better ensure that you hold an instance that isn't a temporary; it's not 100%, but ought to prevent unintentional misuse.

Adding a reference for the duration of the proxy object is another option.  I'm not dead set against it, but I don't really see a huge advantage here, either.

(Also see #599, #617, and #746 for the (rather lengthy) discussion that led up to this; there were multiple proposals for unchecked access considered before we finally settled on the current approach.)

The reason that ``forcecast`` is there now is really my fault and perhaps a bit of personal bias. I work in the area of computer graphics, where 32 bit floats are usually a quite reasonable precision. Many graphics-type projects will involve single precision arrays -- however, Python floats are 64 bit by default. This causes various annoyances without the ``forcecast`` default since it is basically impossible to call any functions without coercing either pybind11 or numpy to accept or convert array arguments.

The one issue with an `operator()` on array is the `writable` runtime flag annoyance, and I don't see a feasible way to deal with that since the reference returned by the operator is determined at compile time.  If it returned a non-const reference, it would need a `flags.writable` check, but then wouldn't be usable for read-only code which would probably want to allow passing a non-writable array. if it returns a const reference, it would be annoying limitation that you can only read but not write the array.

The `.unchecked()`/`.mutable_unchecked()` partially addresses the notation, but only while also doing away with dimension checks, requiring a separate proxy object which is mildly inconvenient, and requiring the caller not to modify the referenced array's shape while the reference proxy exists.

Supporting this via the `const`ness of `*this` (as suggested above) also won't work cleanly: it's entirely possible for a non-const `array` to be non-writeable (`ensure()`, for example, will reference an existing array if the input is compatible or make a copy if necessary (e.g. for requested contiguity or a required type conversion)).

So while I appreciate the syntactic benefit of having a `a(1,2)` work, the mismatch between numpy runtime writability and C++ compile-time constness doesn't lead to a nice solution.

I'm pretty hesitant to add complexity like this to our manual hooks. Hooks for the manual plugin are a nice way to automate simple tasks and that don't necessitate [writing your own plugin](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/contributing.html#writing-your-own-plugin), but as your needs get more specific and complex, I think that writing a simple authenticator plugin is a better option. Doing so only requires implementing a few functions in Python.

As a potentially even easier alternative, why not just have your hooks always include the longer sleep? It's not as efficient, but especially when it'll usually be run non-interactively by something like cron, does the longer wait time really matter in your use case?

TPOT will print everything (all caught warning and exception) when evaluating pipelines or when generating pipelines via randomly creator in initial generation or crossover/mutation in later generations. But most of warnings/exceptions are from scikit-learn operators/TPOT builtin operators and they should be raised in 3 cases:
1. invalid parameter combinations can be randomly generated via creator/crossover/mutation, so TPOT has a pre_test decorator to exclude them before evaluation on training dataset
2. feature selection step in a pipeline is too restricted to allow any feature pass to next step
3. input/intermediate features format issue, like `X needs to contain only non-negative integers.`, which means the input/intermediate features passed to one-hot-encoder operators should only contain only non-negative integers presenting categorical variables. 

Those warning/exceptions when using 3 verbose levels is for diagnosis.

I see, it sounds like the main thing you want is to be able to develop from your laptop without having to ssh to a cluster, is that right?

The "head" could run on the cluster, and you could try running a "worker" on your laptop, for example, you can try the following.

On a remote machine, run the following (replacing the node IP address with something more correct).

```
ray start --head --redis-port=6379 --node-ip-address=123.45.67.89
```

On your laptop, run the following (replacing the node IP address with something more correct). Note that `--num-cpus=0` should prevent tasks from being scheduled on your laptop.

```
ray start --redis-address=123.45.67.89:6379 --node-ip-address=98.765.43.21 --num-cpus=0
```

Then run your script on your laptop using

```
ray.init(redis_address="123.45.67.89:6379")
```

**Note:** You'll have to make sure that your laptop and the remote machines can all communicate with each other properly (in both directions) using the provided IP addresses.

**Note:** I manually specified the node IP addresses because otherwise Ray will try to determine the node IP addresses itself using `ray.services.get_node_ip_address()`. On EC2 instances, this will find the node's private IP address, which your laptop will then use when trying to pull objects from the cluster, but it presumably needs to use the node's public IP address.

Sorry my misexplanation. Of course the Filterset won't be done with `/form1/options/selectfield1` but 
with `/form1/options/selectfield1?selectfield1=A&selectfield2=X&selectfield3=N`. Where `A`, `X` and `N` are choices made in the form. 

A Filterset class for a formfield options class should consist of the ForeignKey model class and filter definitions that translate the selectfield to the reverse fields of the intermediate model class.
e.g. the FilterSet class for selectfield1 would look like:

```
class SelectField1FilterSet(FilterSet):
    selectfield2 = filters.CharFilter(name='intermediatemodel__selectfield2')
    selectfield3 = filters.CharFilter(name='intermediatemodel__selectfield3')
    
    class Meta: 
        model = SelectField1
        fields = ('selectfield2',
                  'selectfield3')
```

Of course `selectfield1` in the query above is not neccessary and is silently dropped by this FilterSet.

Poorly organized thoughts on server API:

`Listener` is an `AsyncResource` with `start` (returns an arbitrary value, does setup, like `__init__` but async) and `accept` (returns an arbitrary value that is usually a `Stream`, all we actually care about is that it has a `forceful_close` method). `start_server` starts the listener, spawns a task to loop on `accept`, and returns `(start_return_value, task_object)`. The former is to let you get back information from the setup code (example: what port are you actually listening on?), and the latter is b/c fancy supervisors will need to know it, to know which thing needs restarting when a task dies. (Or I guess the return value could be some information on the listener object, and we could pass back the listener object itself directly? Or return a `Server` object with a `.listener` attribute and stuff like close methods?)

Maybe `start_server` should take a mandatory timeout argument (can be `inf` if you want), use `graceful_close` on the passed in strings, and pass in a cancel scope to every callback along with the stream? Because if you *need* to have a timeout on network code, and you *need* that timeout to encompass the `graceful_close` call, then every connection callback has to start with a double-indent boilerplate `with open_cancel_scope: async with stream: ...`, and who wants that?

It should also optionally let you set the listener nursery and the connection nursery separately.

Implementations: `TCPListener`, `UnixListener` (I guess this is ambiguous between STREAM and DATAGRAM sockets... maybe Accepter or StreamListener would be better?), `SSLListener` (wraps another listener), probably `HAProxyListener` eventually (need some way to pass back peername etc. on the stream – maybe `SocketStream` should have methods that return `(type, family, proto, python-style sockaddr)`?), ...

Convenience functions: `start_tcp_server` (with optional SSL), `start_unix_server` (with optional SSL?)

> My understanding is that C-extensions are shared across the sub-interpreters, and because of this the exact way to develop with sub-interpreters in mind is a non-trivial affair (and an improvement the subject of a PEP under discussion - https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0554/).

The complexity depends on what you are trying to do, but I agree for many things it may be non-trivial. Most of my experience has been digging in numpy which has problems because they keep static references in c to python objects. When a python interpreter is closed these objects become invalid and fail if numpy is used again. [PEP-3121](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-3121/) was intended to provide extension writers away around this particular problem but it isn't widely used. Pep-554 is interesting so I'll be curious to see how that changes things in the future.

I'm not one of the authors, but I think I know what the problem is.

Killed like that in the text usually means the process was killed by the OS most likely due to being out of memory though I believe depending on kernel configuration a similar message also can be seen if cpu is exhausted.

I would recommend hunting down the OS logs which if it was the OOM memory killer will have a log of it.  I'm not 100% certain if you would see the message on the host or within the docker container, but one of them should have a log of it.
